Test 12: 3D Bin Packing - Polyhedra in Box (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Bin Packing problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: Small containers (5x5x5), simple polyhedra
- **Medium**: Medium containers (10x10x10-20x20x20)
- **Large**: Large containers (50x50x50-100x100x100)
- **Extreme**: Massive containers (200x200x200-500x500x500)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V F (vertex/face count of the polyhedron)
Next V lines: x y z (polyhedron vertices)
Next F lines: a b c (polyhedron face indices)
Last line: W H D (container width, height, depth)
Container origin at (0,0,0), extends to (W,H,D).

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: count (number of polyhedra packed)
Next count lines: tx ty tz qw qx qy qz
tx,ty,tz: translation
qw,qx,qy,qz: rotation quaternion (w is scalar part)

**Quaternion Rotation Reference:**
- Identity (no rotation): 1 0 0 0
- 90° around Z: 0.707 0 0 0.707
- To rotate a point: p' = q * p * q^(-1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container size and polyhedron complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize packing count while ensuring valid placements

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (.NET SDK 10.0.201; SDKs 10.0.201) | csc 5.3.0-2.26153.122 (4d3023de605a78ba3e59e50c657eed70f125c68a) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

View chain of thought

Pack as many copies of an irregular 3D shape into a box as possible, without any overlaps or pieces poking outside. The shapes can be rotated freely, so finding the best orientations is part of the puzzle.

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and spatial reasoning. Subpasses use increasingly awkward shapes that resist neat stacking. The baseline uses a simple grid placement without rotation optimisation.

using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using System.Diagnostics;
using System.IO;

public class Vector3
{
    public double X, Y, Z;
    public Vector3(double x = 0, double y = 0, double z = 0) { X = x; Y = y; Z = z; }
    public static Vector3 operator +(Vector3 a, Vector3 b) => new Vector3(a.X + b.X, a.Y + b.Y, a.Z + b.Z);
    public static Vector3 operator -(Vector3 a, Vector3 b) => new Vector3(a.X - b.X, a.Y - b.Y, a.Z - b.Z);
    public static Vector3 operator *(double s, Vector3 v) => new Vector3(s * v.X, s * v.Y, s * v.Z);
    public static Vector3 operator *(Vector3 v, double s) => s * v;
    public double Dot(Vector3 b) => X * b.X + Y * b.Y + Z * b.Z;
    public Vector3 Cross(Vector3 b) => new Vector3(Y * b.Z - Z * b.Y, Z * b.X - X * b.Z, X * b.Y - Y * b.X);
    public double Length() => Math.Sqrt(X * X + Y * Y + Z * Z);
    public Vector3 Normalized() { var l = Length(); return l > 1e-10 ? (1.0 / l) * this : this; }
    public override string ToString() => $"{X:F3},{Y:F3},{Z:F3}";
}

public class Quaternion
{
    public double W, X, Y, Z;
    public Quaternion(double w = 1, double x = 0, double y = 0, double z = 0) { W = w; X = x; Y = y; Z = z; }
    public Quaternion Normalized()
    {
        var len = Math.Sqrt(W * W + X * X + Y * Y + Z * Z);
        return len > 1e-10 ? new Quaternion(W / len, X / len, Y / len, Z / len) : this;
    }
    public Vector3 RotatePoint(Vector3 p)
    {
        var q = Normalized();
        var qc = new Quaternion(q.W, -q.X, -q.Y, -q.Z);
        var qp = new Quaternion(0, p.X, p.Y, p.Z);
        var res = Mul(Mul(q, qp), qc);
        return new Vector3(res.X, res.Y, res.Z);
    }
    static Quaternion Mul(Quaternion a, Quaternion b) => new Quaternion(
        a.W * b.W - a.X * b.X - a.Y * b.Y - a.Z * b.Z,
        a.W * b.X + a.X * b.W + a.Y * b.Z - a.Z * b.Y,
        a.W * b.Y - a.X * b.Z + a.Y * b.W + a.Z * b.X,
        a.W * b.Z + a.X * b.Y - a.Y * b.X + a.Z * b.W);
    public override string ToString() => $"{W:F6} {X:F6} {Y:F6} {Z:F6}";
}

public class Polyhedron
{
    public List<Vector3> Vertices;
    public List<(int, int, int)> Faces;
    public Vector3 Min, Max;
    public Polyhedron(List<Vector3> vertices, List<(int, int, int)> faces)
    {
        Vertices = vertices;
        Faces = faces;
        ComputeBounds();
    }
    void ComputeBounds()
    {
        Min = new Vector3(Vertices[0].X, Vertices[0].Y, Vertices[0].Z);
        Max = new Vector3(Vertices[0].X, Vertices[0].Y, Vertices[0].Z);
        foreach (var v in Vertices)
        {
            Min.X = Math.Min(Min.X, v.X); Max.X = Math.Max(Max.X, v.X);
            Min.Y = Math.Min(Min.Y, v.Y); Max.Y = Math.Max(Max.Y, v.Y);
            Min.Z = Math.Min(Min.Z, v.Z); Max.Z = Math.Max(Max.Z, v.Z);
        }
    }
    public (Vector3, Vector3) GetTransformedBounds(Vector3 pos, Quaternion rot)
    {
        var min = new Vector3(double.MaxValue, double.MaxValue, double.MaxValue);
        var max = new Vector3(double.MinValue, double.MinValue, double.MinValue);
        foreach (var v in Vertices)
        {
            var rotated = rot.RotatePoint(v);
            var transformed = pos + rotated;
            min.X = Math.Min(min.X, transformed.X); max.X = Math.Max(max.X, transformed.X);
            min.Y = Math.Min(min.Y, transformed.Y); max.Y = Math.Max(max.Y, transformed.Y);
            min.Z = Math.Min(min.Z, transformed.Z); max.Z = Math.Max(max.Z, transformed.Z);
        }
        return (min, max);
    }
}

public class Placement
{
    public Vector3 Position;
    public Quaternion Rotation;
    public Placement(Vector3 pos, Quaternion rot) { Position = pos; Rotation = rot; }
}

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        var timer = Stopwatch.StartNew();

        var input = Console.In.ReadToEnd().Trim().Split('\n');
        var parts = input[0].Split(' ');
        int V = int.Parse(parts[0]), F = int.Parse(parts[1]);

        var vertices = new List<Vector3>();
        for (int i = 0; i < V; i++)
        {
            var coords = input[1 + i].Split(' ');
            vertices.Add(new Vector3(double.Parse(coords[0]), double.Parse(coords[1]), double.Parse(coords[2])));
        }

        var faces = new List<(int, int, int)>();
        for (int i = 0; i < F; i++)
        {
            var indices = input[1 + V + i].Split(' ');
            faces.Add((int.Parse(indices[0]), int.Parse(indices[1]), int.Parse(indices[2])));
        }

        var dimParts = input[1 + V + F].Split(' ');
        int W = int.Parse(dimParts[0]), H = int.Parse(dimParts[1]), D = int.Parse(dimParts[2]);

        var poly = new Polyhedron(vertices, faces);
        var placements = new List<Placement>();

        var rotations = GenerateRotations(W, H, D);
        var step = EstimateGridStep(W, H, D);

        for (double x = 0; x < W && timer.ElapsedMilliseconds < 290000; x += step)
            for (double y = 0; y < H && timer.ElapsedMilliseconds < 290000; y += step)
                for (double z = 0; z < D && timer.ElapsedMilliseconds < 290000; z += step)
                    foreach (var rot in rotations)
                    {
                        var pos = new Vector3(x, y, z);
                        if (CanPlace(poly, pos, rot, placements, W, H, D))
                            placements.Add(new Placement(pos, rot));
                    }

        Console.WriteLine(placements.Count);
        foreach (var p in placements)
            Console.WriteLine($"{p.Position.X:F1} {p.Position.Y:F1} {p.Position.Z:F1} {p.Rotation}");
    }

    static List<Quaternion> GenerateRotations(int W, int H, int D)
    {
        var rots = new List<Quaternion> { new Quaternion(1, 0, 0, 0) };
        var size = Math.Max(W, Math.Max(H, D));

        if (size < 50)
        {
            for (int deg = 45; deg < 360; deg += 45)
            {
                double rad = deg * Math.PI / 180;
                double s = Math.Sin(rad / 2);
                rots.Add(new Quaternion(Math.Cos(rad / 2), s, 0, 0));
                rots.Add(new Quaternion(Math.Cos(rad / 2), 0, s, 0));
                rots.Add(new Quaternion(Math.Cos(rad / 2), 0, 0, s));
            }
        }
        else if (size < 100)
        {
            for (int deg = 90; deg < 360; deg += 90)
            {
                double rad = deg * Math.PI / 180;
                double s = Math.Sin(rad / 2);
                rots.Add(new Quaternion(Math.Cos(rad / 2), s, 0, 0));
                rots.Add(new Quaternion(Math.Cos(rad / 2), 0, s, 0));
                rots.Add(new Quaternion(Math.Cos(rad / 2), 0, 0, s));
            }
        }

        return rots;
    }

    static double EstimateGridStep(int W, int H, int D)
    {
        var maxDim = Math.Max(W, Math.Max(H, D));
        if (maxDim < 20) return 1.0;
        if (maxDim < 50) return 2.0;
        if (maxDim < 100) return 5.0;
        return Math.Max(10.0, maxDim / 20.0);
    }

    static bool CanPlace(Polyhedron poly, Vector3 pos, Quaternion rot, List<Placement> placed, int W, int H, int D)
    {
        var (min, max) = poly.GetTransformedBounds(pos, rot);

        if (min.X < 0 || max.X > W || min.Y < 0 || max.Y > H || min.Z < 0 || max.Z > D)
            return false;

        foreach (var p in placed)
        {
            if (Overlaps(poly, pos, rot, p.Position, p.Rotation))
                return false;
        }

        return true;
    }

    static bool Overlaps(Polyhedron poly, Vector3 pos1, Quaternion rot1, Vector3 pos2, Quaternion rot2)
    {
        var (min1, max1) = poly.GetTransformedBounds(pos1, rot1);
        var (min2, max2) = poly.GetTransformedBounds(pos2, rot2);

        return !(max1.X < min2.X || max2.X < min1.X ||
                 max1.Y < min2.Y || max2.Y < min1.Y ||
                 max1.Z < min2.Z || max2.Z < min1.Z);
    }
}

Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Longest Common Substring problem in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-3 short strings (10-50 chars each)
- **Medium**: 3-5 medium strings (100-500 chars each)
- **Large**: 5-10 long strings (1000-5000 chars each)
- **Extreme**: 100 very long strings (10MB+ each), massive search space

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of strings)
Next N lines: one string per line

**Output format (stdout):**
One line: the longest common substring
If no common substring exists, output an empty line.

**Example:**
Input:
3
abcdef
xbcdey
zbcdew

Output:
bcde

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and length of strings
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for very long strings
3. **Correctness**: Must find the actual longest common substring

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Given several strings, find the longest chunk of text that appears identically in every one of them. For example, if the strings are “abcdef” and “xbcdey”, the answer is “bcde”.

Naïve approaches check every possible substring and become far too slow on large inputs. Subpasses increase the string lengths into the millions, so the AI must use efficient data structures or hashing. The baseline uses a simple library call that times out on the bigger cases.

 0.0000
[3 short strings (7 chars)] Invalid: Result must be string, got dict

Longest Common Substring - 3 short strings (7 chars)

View Code (1986 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

bool existsInAll(const vector<string>& strings, const string& candidate) {
    for (const auto& s : strings) {
        if (s.find(candidate) == string::npos) {
            return false;
        }
    }
    return true;
}

string findLCS(const vector<string>& strings) {
    if (strings.empty()) return "";
    if (strings.size() == 1) return strings[0];

    // Find the shortest string (limits maximum LCS length)
    size_t minLen = strings[0].size();
    int minIdx = 0;
    for (size_t i = 1; i < strings.size(); i++) {
        if (strings[i].size() < minLen) {
            minLen = strings[i].size();
            minIdx = i;
        }
    }

    const string& refString = strings[minIdx];
    string best = "";

    // Binary search on the length of the result
    // This reduces iterations from O(n) to O(log n)
    size_t left = 0, right = minLen;
    while (left <= right) {
        size_t mid = (left + right) / 2;
        bool found = false;

        // Try all substrings of length mid in the reference string
        for (size_t start = 0; start + mid <= minLen; start++) {
            string candidate = refString.substr(start, mid);
            if (existsInAll(strings, candidate)) {
                best = candidate;
                found = true;
                break;  // Found one valid substring of this length
            }
        }

        if (found) {
            // Try longer substrings
            left = mid + 1;
        } else {
            // Try shorter substrings
            right = (mid == 0) ? 0 : mid - 1;
        }
    }

    return best;
}

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n;
    cin >> n;
    cin.ignore();

    vector<string> strings(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        getline(cin, strings[i]);
    }

    string result = findLCS(strings);
    cout << result << endl;

    return 0;
}

Test 14: Minesweeper Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 10
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a Minesweeper solver in C# that must handle ANY board complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

- **Trivial**: Small boards (5x5-8x8), simple patterns, few mines
- **Extreme**: Massive boards (10000x10000), very complex constraint satisfaction problems

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the board row strings (each W characters)

**Cell meanings:**
- ' ' (space): Unknown/unrevealed cell - could be mine or safe
- '0'-'8': Revealed safe cell showing count of adjacent mines (8 neighbors)
- '*': Flagged mine (known mine location)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of safe cells found)
Next N lines: x y (column row, 0-indexed)
Only output cells that are currently unknown (' ') and provably safe.
Do NOT guess - only return cells you can prove are safe.

**Example:**
For a board where cell (0,0) shows '1' and has one adjacent mine at (1,0):
Neighbors (0,1) and (0,2) are guaranteed safe.

**Constraints:**
- Return as many provably safe cells as possible
- Never return a cell that could be a mine
- Must handle varying board sizes and mine densities efficiently

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Given a partially-revealed Minesweeper board, deduce which hidden cells are safe to click and which contain mines. Each visible number tells you exactly how many of its neighbours are mined — the AI must combine these clues to make guaranteed-safe moves without guessing.

Larger boards and denser mine layouts make the logic harder. The problem is NP-complete in general, but clever constraint reasoning can solve most practical boards. The baseline uses only the simplest single-cell deduction rules.

Minesweeper Solver - 8x8 board, 10 mines

View Code (4284 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

class MinesweeperSolver
{
    private char[,] board;
    private bool[,] isSafe;
    private bool[,] isMine;
    private int height;
    private int width;

    static void Main()
    {
        var solver = new MinesweeperSolver();
        solver.Solve();
    }

    void Solve()
    {
        // Read input
        string[] firstLine = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        height = int.Parse(firstLine[0]);
        width = int.Parse(firstLine[1]);

        board = new char[height, width];
        isSafe = new bool[height, width];
        isMine = new bool[height, width];

        for (int i = 0; i < height; i++)
        {
            string line = Console.ReadLine();
            for (int j = 0; j < width; j++)
            {
                board[i, j] = j < line.Length ? line[j] : ' ';
                if (board[i, j] == '*')
                    isMine[i, j] = true;
            }
        }

        // Constraint propagation
        ConstraintPropagation();

        // Collect safe cells that are unknown and provably safe
        var safeCells = new List<(int, int)>();
        for (int i = 0; i < height; i++)
        {
            for (int j = 0; j < width; j++)
            {
                if (board[i, j] == ' ' && isSafe[i, j])
                    safeCells.Add((j, i)); // Output as (x, y) = (col, row)
            }
        }

        // Output
        Console.WriteLine(safeCells.Count);
        foreach (var (x, y) in safeCells.OrderBy(c => c.Item1).ThenBy(c => c.Item2))
        {
            Console.WriteLine($"{x} {y}");
        }
    }

    void ConstraintPropagation()
    {
        bool changed = true;
        int iterations = 0;
        int maxIterations = height + width;

        while (changed && iterations < maxIterations)
        {
            changed = false;
            iterations++;

            // Process each numbered cell
            for (int i = 0; i < height; i++)
            {
                for (int j = 0; j < width; j++)
                {
                    if (char.IsDigit(board[i, j]))
                    {
                        if (ProcessCell(i, j))
                            changed = true;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }

    bool ProcessCell(int row, int col)
    {
        int requiredMines = board[row, col] - '0';
        var neighbors = GetNeighbors(row, col);

        int knownMines = 0;
        int unknowns = 0;
        var unknownCells = new List<(int, int)>();

        // Count known mines and unknown cells
        foreach (var (nr, nc) in neighbors)
        {
            if (isMine[nr, nc])
            {
                knownMines++;
            }
            else if (board[nr, nc] == ' ' && !isSafe[nr, nc])
            {
                unknowns++;
                unknownCells.Add((nr, nc));
            }
        }

        bool changed = false;
        int remainingMines = requiredMines - knownMines;

        // If all mines are accounted for, all remaining unknowns are safe
        if (remainingMines == 0)
        {
            foreach (var (nr, nc) in unknownCells)
            {
                if (!isSafe[nr, nc])
                {
                    isSafe[nr, nc] = true;
                    changed = true;
                }
            }
        }
        // If unknowns exactly equal remaining mines, all unknowns are mines
        else if (remainingMines == unknowns && unknowns > 0)
        {
            foreach (var (nr, nc) in unknownCells)
            {
                if (!isMine[nr, nc])
                {
                    isMine[nr, nc] = true;
                    changed = true;
                }
            }
        }

        return changed;
    }

    List<(int, int)> GetNeighbors(int row, int col)
    {
        var neighbors = new List<(int, int)>();
        for (int dr = -1; dr <= 1; dr++)
        {
            for (int dc = -1; dc <= 1; dc++)
            {
                if (dr == 0 && dc == 0) continue;
                int nr = row + dr;
                int nc = col + dc;
                if (nr >= 0 && nr < height && nc >= 0 && nc < width)
                    neighbors.Add((nr, nc));
            }
        }
        return neighbors;
    }
}

Test 15: Tetrahedron Shadow Covering (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Shadow Covering problem with tetrahedrons in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with polygons ranging from simple shapes to very complex geometries.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: NV (number of polygon vertices)
Next NV lines: x y (polygon vertices on z=0 plane)
Last line: sx sy sz (sun direction vector, points from sun toward ground)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: count (number of tetrahedrons)
Next count lines: px py pz qw qx qy qz scale
px,py,pz: center position
qw,qx,qy,qz: rotation quaternion (w is scalar)
scale: uniform scale factor

**Standard Tetrahedron Reference:**
Vertices (before transform):
[1.0, 0.0, -0.707]
[-1.0, 0.0, -0.707]
[0.0, 1.0, 0.707]
[0.0, -1.0, 0.707]

**Constraints:**
- Combined shadows must completely cover target polygon
- Tetrahedrons must not intersect each other in 3D
- Maximum scale factor per tetrahedron: 10.0
- Minimize number of tetrahedrons

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Be aware that default warnings are enabled and will cause a compilation failure,
so ensure that you write warning-free code.

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:

View chain of thought

Place 3D tetrahedra (triangular pyramids) above a flat surface so that their shadows, cast by a directional light, completely cover a given 2D target shape. Use as few tetrahedra as possible while covering every part of the target.

The AI must reason about 3D-to-2D projection, rotation, and coverage geometry. Subpasses use more complex target shapes and trickier light angles. The baseline uses a simple grid of tetrahedra above the target.

 0.0000
[3x3 square, sun straight down] 62% coverage with 1 tetrahedrons, Time: 0.1s

Shadow Covering - 3x3 square, sun straight down

View Code (3871 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read number of vertices
    let nv: usize = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap().trim().parse().unwrap();

    // Read polygon vertices
    let mut polygon = Vec::new();
    for _ in 0..nv {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
        let x: f64 = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
        let y: f64 = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
        polygon.push((x, y));
    }

    // Read sun direction
    let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let sun_x: f64 = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let sun_y: f64 = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
    let sun_z: f64 = parts[2].parse().unwrap();

    // Normalize sun direction
    let sun_len = (sun_x * sun_x + sun_y * sun_y + sun_z * sun_z).sqrt();
    let _sun = (sun_x / sun_len, sun_y / sun_len, sun_z / sun_len);

    // Standard tetrahedron vertices (before transform)
    let _tet_verts = [
        (1.0, 0.0, -0.707),
        (-1.0, 0.0, -0.707),
        (0.0, 1.0, 0.707),
        (0.0, -1.0, 0.707),
    ];

    // Find polygon bounding box and centroid
    let mut min_x = f64::INFINITY;
    let mut max_x = f64::NEG_INFINITY;
    let mut min_y = f64::INFINITY;
    let mut max_y = f64::NEG_INFINITY;
    let mut center_x = 0.0;
    let mut center_y = 0.0;

    for (x, y) in &polygon {
        min_x = min_x.min(*x);
        max_x = max_x.max(*x);
        min_y = min_y.min(*y);
        max_y = max_y.max(*y);
        center_x += x;
        center_y += y;
    }

    center_x /= polygon.len() as f64;
    center_y /= polygon.len() as f64;

    let width = max_x - min_x;
    let height = max_y - min_y;
    let diag = (width * width + height * height).sqrt();

    // Determine number of tetrahedrons based on polygon complexity
    let num_tets = if polygon.len() <= 4 {
        1
    } else if polygon.len() <= 8 {
        1
    } else if diag > 50.0 {
        ((diag / 30.0).ceil() as usize).min(5)
    } else {
        1
    };

    let mut tetrahedrons = Vec::new();

    if num_tets == 1 {
        // Single tetrahedron positioned above polygon center
        let z_pos = (diag / 2.0).max(2.0);
        let scale = (diag / 2.5).min(10.0).max(0.5);

        tetrahedrons.push((center_x, center_y, z_pos, 1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0, scale));
    } else {
        // Multiple tetrahedrons distributed around polygon
        let grid_size = (num_tets as f64).sqrt().ceil() as usize;
        let step_x = (width + 4.0) / (grid_size as f64);
        let step_y = (height + 4.0) / (grid_size as f64);
        let scale = (diag / (3.0 * (grid_size as f64))).min(10.0).max(0.3);

        let mut count = 0;
        for i in 0..grid_size {
            for j in 0..grid_size {
                if count >= num_tets {
                    break;
                }
                let x = min_x - 2.0 + (i as f64) * step_x;
                let y = min_y - 2.0 + (j as f64) * step_y;
                let z = (diag / 3.0).max(1.5);

                // Vary quaternion for different rotations based on grid position
                let angle = (i as f64 + j as f64) * 0.5;
                let qw = angle.cos();
                let qx = (angle * 0.7).sin();
                let qy = (angle * 0.3).sin();
                let qz = (angle * 0.5).sin();

                let len = (qw * qw + qx * qx + qy * qy + qz * qz).sqrt();
                tetrahedrons.push((x, y, z, qw / len, qx / len, qy / len, qz / len, scale));

                count += 1;
            }
        }
    }

    // Output result
    println!("{}", tetrahedrons.len());
    for (px, py, pz, qw, qx, qy, qz, scale) in tetrahedrons {
        println!("{} {} {} {} {} {} {} {}", px, py, pz, qw, qx, qy, qz, scale);
    }
}

Test 16: Job-Shop Scheduling (C++) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Job-Shop Scheduling problem in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to extremely large scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-3 jobs, 2-3 machines, 5-10 tasks total
- **Extreme**: 1000+ jobs, 50+ machines, 50k+ tasks total

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: J M (number of jobs, number of machines)
For each job:
Line: T (number of tasks in this job)
Next T lines: machine duration

**Output format (stdout):**
For each job (J lines total):
T pairs per line: start_time machine start_time machine ... (all tasks for that job)

**Example:**
Input:
2 2
2
0 3
1 2
2
1 2
0 4

Output:
0 0 3 1
0 1 5 0

(Job 0: task 0 starts at t=0 on M0, task 1 at t=3 on M1)
(Job 1: task 0 starts at t=0 on M1, task 1 at t=5 on M0)

**Key Constraints:**
1. Each task in a job must run on its specified machine
2. Tasks within a job must execute in order (task i before task i+1)
3. A machine can only run one task at a time
4. Tasks cannot be interrupted once started

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Schedule a set of jobs across several machines to finish everything as quickly as possible. Each job is a sequence of tasks that must run in order, and each task needs a specific machine — but a machine can only do one thing at a time.

This classic factory-scheduling problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number of jobs and machines. The baseline greedily starts whichever task can begin earliest.

 0.0000
[3 jobs, 2 machines] Makespan: 16, Baseline: 12, Time: 0.5s - valid but inefficient (1.33x baseline)

Job-Shop Scheduling - 3 jobs, 2 machines

View Code (1929 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>

int main() {
    int J, M;
    std::cin >> J >> M;

    // Read job definitions: for each job, read task count and task specifications
    std::vector<std::vector<std::pair<int, int>>> jobs(J);
    int total_tasks = 0;
    for (int j = 0; j < J; j++) {
        int T;
        std::cin >> T;
        total_tasks += T;
        for (int t = 0; t < T; t++) {
            int machine, duration;
            std::cin >> machine >> duration;
            jobs[j].push_back({machine, duration});
        }
    }

    // Adaptive strategy selection based on problem scale
    // For small instances: could apply local search optimization
    // For large instances: stick with efficient greedy approach
    bool is_small = (J <= 10 && M <= 10 && total_tasks <= 100);
    bool is_large = (J > 100 || M > 20 || total_tasks > 5000);

    // Track when each machine becomes available
    std::vector<int> machine_free_time(M, 0);

    // Schedule each job's tasks in sequence
    for (int j = 0; j < J; j++) {
        int job_free_time = 0;  // When previous task in this job finishes

        for (size_t t = 0; t < jobs[j].size(); t++) {
            int machine = jobs[j][t].first;
            int duration = jobs[j][t].second;

            // Task starts when both:
            // 1. Previous task in job finishes (job_free_time)
            // 2. Required machine becomes available (machine_free_time[machine])
            int start_time = std::max(job_free_time, machine_free_time[machine]);

            // Output this task's schedule: start_time and machine_id
            if (t > 0) std::cout << " ";
            std::cout << start_time << " " << machine;

            // Update availability times
            int end_time = start_time + duration;
            machine_free_time[machine] = end_time;
            job_free_time = end_time;
        }
        std::cout << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Bin Packing problem with axis-aligned boxes in C#.

Your program must work on all scales, from 10,000 boxes in 1,000,000 cubic units
down to 4 boxes in a 10x10x10 container.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N W H D (number of boxes, container width, height, depth)
Next N lines: w h d (box dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: packed_count
Next packed_count lines: box_index x y z (0-indexed box index and corner position)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container size and number of boxes
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for very large containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of boxes packed while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Boxes must be entirely within container bounds
- No two boxes may overlap
- Boxes are axis-aligned (no arbitrary rotation, but you may reorder w/h/d)
- Maximize number of boxes packed

Pack a collection of rectangular boxes into a shipping container, fitting as much total volume in as possible. Boxes may be rotated to any axis-aligned orientation, but cannot overlap or stick out of the container.

This 3D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of box sizes. The baseline uses a greedy first-fit approach.

 0.0000
[4 boxes in 10x10x10] Compilation failed:
Determining projects to restore...
Restored D:\CodingBenchmark\compile_cache\claude-haiku-4-5-LowReasoning\csharp\project_e5582aeeedb75350\Program.csproj (in 176 ms).
CSC : err

3D AABB Packing - 4 boxes in 10x10x10

View Code (4978 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

class Program
{
    struct Box
    {
        public int Index, W, H, D;
        public long Volume => (long)W * H * D;
    }

    struct FreeRect
    {
        public int X, Y, Z, W, H, D;
    }

    struct Placement
    {
        public int Index, X, Y, Z;
    }

    static void Main()
    {
        var line1 = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        int n = int.Parse(line1[0]);
        int containerW = int.Parse(line1[1]);
        int containerH = int.Parse(line1[2]);
        int containerD = int.Parse(line1[3]);

        var boxes = new Box[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            var dims = Console.ReadLine().Split();
            boxes[i] = new Box
            {
                Index = i,
                W = int.Parse(dims[0]),
                H = int.Parse(dims[1]),
                D = int.Parse(dims[2])
            };
        }

        var placements = new List<Placement>();
        var freeRects = new List<FreeRect>
        {
            new FreeRect { X = 0, Y = 0, Z = 0, W = containerW, H = containerH, D = containerD }
        };

        // Sort boxes by volume (descending) for better packing
        var sortedBoxes = boxes.OrderByDescending(b => b.Volume).ToList();

        foreach (var box in sortedBoxes)
        {
            // Generate all unique orientations
            var orientations = GetOrientations(box.W, box.H, box.D);
            int bestRectIdx = -1;
            (int w, int h, int d) bestDims = (0, 0, 0);
            int bestWaste = int.MaxValue;

            // Find best fitting rectangle and orientation
            for (int rectIdx = 0; rectIdx < freeRects.Count; rectIdx++)
            {
                var rect = freeRects[rectIdx];

                foreach (var (w, h, d) in orientations)
                {
                    if (w <= rect.W && h <= rect.H && d <= rect.D)
                    {
                        // Prefer rectangle with least waste
                        int waste = rect.W * rect.H * rect.D - w * h * d;
                        if (waste < bestWaste)
                        {
                            bestWaste = waste;
                            bestRectIdx = rectIdx;
                            bestDims = (w, h, d);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }

            // Place the box if we found a suitable rectangle
            if (bestRectIdx >= 0)
            {
                var rect = freeRects[bestRectIdx];
                placements.Add(new Placement { Index = box.Index, X = rect.X, Y = rect.Y, Z = rect.Z });

                // Remove the used rectangle
                freeRects.RemoveAt(bestRectIdx);

                // Add new free rectangles from guillotine splits
                if (bestDims.w < rect.W)
                    freeRects.Add(new FreeRect
                    {
                        X = rect.X + bestDims.w,
                        Y = rect.Y,
                        Z = rect.Z,
                        W = rect.W - bestDims.w,
                        H = rect.H,
                        D = rect.D
                    });

                if (bestDims.h < rect.H)
                    freeRects.Add(new FreeRect
                    {
                        X = rect.X,
                        Y = rect.Y + bestDims.h,
                        Z = rect.Z,
                        W = bestDims.w,
                        H = rect.H - bestDims.h,
                        D = rect.D
                    });

                if (bestDims.d < rect.D)
                    freeRects.Add(new FreeRect
                    {
                        X = rect.X,
                        Y = rect.Y,
                        Z = rect.Z + bestDims.d,
                        W = bestDims.w,
                        H = bestDims.h,
                        D = rect.D - bestDims.d
                    });

                // Remove invalid rectangles (those that don't fit the constraints)
                freeRects = freeRects.Where(r => r.W > 0 && r.H > 0 && r.D > 0).ToList();
            }
        }

        // Output results
        Console.WriteLine(placements.Count);
        foreach (var p in placements.OrderBy(x => x.Index))
        {
            Console.WriteLine($"{p.Index} {p.X} {p.Y} {p.Z}");
        }
    }

    static List<(int, int, int)> GetOrientations(int w, int h, int d)
    {
        var orientations = new HashSet<(int, int, int)>();
        var dims = new[] { w, h, d };

        // Generate all permutations
        var perms = new[] {
            (dims[0], dims[1], dims[2]),
            (dims[0], dims[2], dims[1]),
            (dims[1], dims[0], dims[2]),
            (dims[1], dims[2], dims[0]),
            (dims[2], dims[0], dims[1]),
            (dims[2], dims[1], dims[0])
        };

        foreach (var perm in perms)
            orientations.Add(perm);

        return orientations.ToList();
    }
}

Test 18: Minimum Cut Problem (C++) Score: 3.00 / 9
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a C++ program to solve the Minimum Edge Cut problem.

**Target environment specs**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler information**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to multi-gb.

**The Challenge:**
Your C++ program will be tested with graphs ranging from small toy examples to massive real-world networks.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Problem:**
Given an undirected connected graph, find the minimum number of edges to remove
such that the graph becomes disconnected (split into two non-empty components).

**Input format (stdin):**
- Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
- Lines 2 to M+1: u v (undirected edge between nodes u and v, 0-indexed)

**Output format (stdout):**
- Line 1: K (number of edges in the minimum cut)
- Lines 2 to K+1: u v (each edge in the cut, in any order)

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with this compiler, and run on this environment.
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle graphs even if they don't fit in memory.
4. Complete as fast as possible.
5. Output must be a valid cut that disconnects the graph
6. Minimize the number of cut edges
7. Must handle varying graph sizes efficiently

**Example:**
Input:
```
4 5
0 1
1 2
2 3
3 0
0 2
```

Output (one possible answer):
```
2
0 1
3 0
```

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different algorithms based on graph scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find the smallest set of edges to remove from a graph so that it splits into two disconnected pieces. Think of it like finding the weakest point in a network — the fewest cables you’d need to cut to divide it in two.

Subpasses increase the graph size from small networks to very large ones. The AI must implement an efficient algorithm; naïve approaches are far too slow on the bigger inputs.

 1.0000
[6 nodes, 10 edges, min cut ~2] Cut size: 1, Known min: 2, Time: 0.57s - optimal

Subpass 0: 6 nodes, 10 edges, min cut ~2

Score: 1.00

Details: [6 nodes, 10 edges, min cut ~2] Cut size: 1, Known min: 2, Time: 0.57s - optimal

C++ Code 
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <algorithm>
#include <cstring>

using namespace std;

const int INF = 1e9;

struct Edge {
    int to, cap, flow;
};

class MaxFlow {
public:
    vector<Edge> edges;
    vector<vector<int>> g;
    vector<int> level, iter;
    int n;

    MaxFlow(int n) : n(n), g(n), level(n), iter(n) {}

    void addEdge(int from, int to, int cap) {
        g[from].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({to, cap, 0});
        g[to].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({from, 0, 0});
    }

    bool bfs(int s, int t) {
        fill(level.begin(), level.end(), -1);
        level[s] = 0;
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                if (level[edges[id].to] < 0 && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    level[edges[id].to] = level[v] + 1;
                    q.push(edges[id].to);
                }
            }
        }

        return level[t] >= 0;
    }

    int dfs(int v, int t, int pushed) {
        if (v == t || pushed == 0) return pushed;

        for (int& cid = iter[v]; cid < (int)g[v].size(); cid++) {
            int id = g[v][cid];
            int to = edges[id].to;

            if (level[v] + 1 != level[to] || edges[id].cap <= edges[id].flow)
                continue;

            int tr = dfs(to, t, min(pushed, edges[id].cap - edges[id].flow));
            if (tr > 0) {
                edges[id].flow += tr;
                edges[id ^ 1].flow -= tr;
                return tr;
            }
        }

        return 0;
    }

    int maxflow(int s, int t) {
        int flow = 0;
        while (bfs(s, t)) {
            fill(iter.begin(), iter.end(), 0);
            while (int pushed = dfs(s, t, INF)) {
                flow += pushed;
            }
        }
        return flow;
    }

    vector<bool> getSourceSide(int s) {
        vector<bool> reachable(n, false);
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);
        reachable[s] = true;

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                int to = edges[id].to;
                if (!reachable[to] && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    reachable[to] = true;
                    q.push(to);
                }
            }
        }

        return reachable;
    }
};

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;

    vector<pair<int, int>> edges(m);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        edges[i] = {u, v};
    }

    if (n <= 1) {
        cout << 0 << "\n";
        return 0;
    }

    int minCut = INF;
    int bestTarget = 1;

    for (int t = 1; t < n; t++) {
        MaxFlow mf(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
        }

        int flow = mf.maxflow(0, t);
        if (flow < minCut) {
            minCut = flow;
            bestTarget = t;
        }

        if (minCut == 1) break;
    }

    MaxFlow mf(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
    }

    mf.maxflow(0, bestTarget);

    vector<bool> sourceSide = mf.getSourceSide(0);

    vector<pair<int, int>> cutEdges;
    for (auto [u, v] : edges) {
        if (sourceSide[u] && !sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({u, v});
        } else if (!sourceSide[u] && sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({v, u});
        }
    }

    cout << minCut << "\n";
    for (auto [u, v] : cutEdges) {
        cout << u << " " << v << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}
Subpass 1
Subpass configurationView exact prompt

Subpass 1: 100 nodes, 400 edges, min cut ~6

Score: 1.00

Details: [100 nodes, 400 edges, min cut ~6] Cut size: 2, Known min: 6, Time: 0.02s - optimal

C++ Code 
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <algorithm>
#include <cstring>

using namespace std;

const int INF = 1e9;

struct Edge {
    int to, cap, flow;
};

class MaxFlow {
public:
    vector<Edge> edges;
    vector<vector<int>> g;
    vector<int> level, iter;
    int n;

    MaxFlow(int n) : n(n), g(n), level(n), iter(n) {}

    void addEdge(int from, int to, int cap) {
        g[from].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({to, cap, 0});
        g[to].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({from, 0, 0});
    }

    bool bfs(int s, int t) {
        fill(level.begin(), level.end(), -1);
        level[s] = 0;
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                if (level[edges[id].to] < 0 && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    level[edges[id].to] = level[v] + 1;
                    q.push(edges[id].to);
                }
            }
        }

        return level[t] >= 0;
    }

    int dfs(int v, int t, int pushed) {
        if (v == t || pushed == 0) return pushed;

        for (int& cid = iter[v]; cid < (int)g[v].size(); cid++) {
            int id = g[v][cid];
            int to = edges[id].to;

            if (level[v] + 1 != level[to] || edges[id].cap <= edges[id].flow)
                continue;

            int tr = dfs(to, t, min(pushed, edges[id].cap - edges[id].flow));
            if (tr > 0) {
                edges[id].flow += tr;
                edges[id ^ 1].flow -= tr;
                return tr;
            }
        }

        return 0;
    }

    int maxflow(int s, int t) {
        int flow = 0;
        while (bfs(s, t)) {
            fill(iter.begin(), iter.end(), 0);
            while (int pushed = dfs(s, t, INF)) {
                flow += pushed;
            }
        }
        return flow;
    }

    vector<bool> getSourceSide(int s) {
        vector<bool> reachable(n, false);
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);
        reachable[s] = true;

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                int to = edges[id].to;
                if (!reachable[to] && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    reachable[to] = true;
                    q.push(to);
                }
            }
        }

        return reachable;
    }
};

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;

    vector<pair<int, int>> edges(m);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        edges[i] = {u, v};
    }

    if (n <= 1) {
        cout << 0 << "\n";
        return 0;
    }

    int minCut = INF;
    int bestTarget = 1;

    for (int t = 1; t < n; t++) {
        MaxFlow mf(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
        }

        int flow = mf.maxflow(0, t);
        if (flow < minCut) {
            minCut = flow;
            bestTarget = t;
        }

        if (minCut == 1) break;
    }

    MaxFlow mf(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
    }

    mf.maxflow(0, bestTarget);

    vector<bool> sourceSide = mf.getSourceSide(0);

    vector<pair<int, int>> cutEdges;
    for (auto [u, v] : edges) {
        if (sourceSide[u] && !sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({u, v});
        } else if (!sourceSide[u] && sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({v, u});
        }
    }

    cout << minCut << "\n";
    for (auto [u, v] : cutEdges) {
        cout << u << " " << v << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

C++ Code 
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <algorithm>
#include <cstring>

using namespace std;

const int INF = 1e9;

struct Edge {
    int to, cap, flow;
};

class MaxFlow {
public:
    vector<Edge> edges;
    vector<vector<int>> g;
    vector<int> level, iter;
    int n;

    MaxFlow(int n) : n(n), g(n), level(n), iter(n) {}

    void addEdge(int from, int to, int cap) {
        g[from].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({to, cap, 0});
        g[to].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({from, 0, 0});
    }

    bool bfs(int s, int t) {
        fill(level.begin(), level.end(), -1);
        level[s] = 0;
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                if (level[edges[id].to] < 0 && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    level[edges[id].to] = level[v] + 1;
                    q.push(edges[id].to);
                }
            }
        }

        return level[t] >= 0;
    }

    int dfs(int v, int t, int pushed) {
        if (v == t || pushed == 0) return pushed;

        for (int& cid = iter[v]; cid < (int)g[v].size(); cid++) {
            int id = g[v][cid];
            int to = edges[id].to;

            if (level[v] + 1 != level[to] || edges[id].cap <= edges[id].flow)
                continue;

            int tr = dfs(to, t, min(pushed, edges[id].cap - edges[id].flow));
            if (tr > 0) {
                edges[id].flow += tr;
                edges[id ^ 1].flow -= tr;
                return tr;
            }
        }

        return 0;
    }

    int maxflow(int s, int t) {
        int flow = 0;
        while (bfs(s, t)) {
            fill(iter.begin(), iter.end(), 0);
            while (int pushed = dfs(s, t, INF)) {
                flow += pushed;
            }
        }
        return flow;
    }

    vector<bool> getSourceSide(int s) {
        vector<bool> reachable(n, false);
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);
        reachable[s] = true;

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                int to = edges[id].to;
                if (!reachable[to] && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    reachable[to] = true;
                    q.push(to);
                }
            }
        }

        return reachable;
    }
};

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;

    vector<pair<int, int>> edges(m);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        edges[i] = {u, v};
    }

    if (n <= 1) {
        cout << 0 << "\n";
        return 0;
    }

    int minCut = INF;
    int bestTarget = 1;

    for (int t = 1; t < n; t++) {
        MaxFlow mf(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
        }

        int flow = mf.maxflow(0, t);
        if (flow < minCut) {
            minCut = flow;
            bestTarget = t;
        }

        if (minCut == 1) break;
    }

    MaxFlow mf(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
    }

    mf.maxflow(0, bestTarget);

    vector<bool> sourceSide = mf.getSourceSide(0);

    vector<pair<int, int>> cutEdges;
    for (auto [u, v] : edges) {
        if (sourceSide[u] && !sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({u, v});
        } else if (!sourceSide[u] && sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({v, u});
        }
    }

    cout << minCut << "\n";
    for (auto [u, v] : cutEdges) {
        cout << u << " " << v << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

C++ Code 
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <algorithm>
#include <cstring>

using namespace std;

const int INF = 1e9;

struct Edge {
    int to, cap, flow;
};

class MaxFlow {
public:
    vector<Edge> edges;
    vector<vector<int>> g;
    vector<int> level, iter;
    int n;

    MaxFlow(int n) : n(n), g(n), level(n), iter(n) {}

    void addEdge(int from, int to, int cap) {
        g[from].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({to, cap, 0});
        g[to].push_back(edges.size());
        edges.push_back({from, 0, 0});
    }

    bool bfs(int s, int t) {
        fill(level.begin(), level.end(), -1);
        level[s] = 0;
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                if (level[edges[id].to] < 0 && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    level[edges[id].to] = level[v] + 1;
                    q.push(edges[id].to);
                }
            }
        }

        return level[t] >= 0;
    }

    int dfs(int v, int t, int pushed) {
        if (v == t || pushed == 0) return pushed;

        for (int& cid = iter[v]; cid < (int)g[v].size(); cid++) {
            int id = g[v][cid];
            int to = edges[id].to;

            if (level[v] + 1 != level[to] || edges[id].cap <= edges[id].flow)
                continue;

            int tr = dfs(to, t, min(pushed, edges[id].cap - edges[id].flow));
            if (tr > 0) {
                edges[id].flow += tr;
                edges[id ^ 1].flow -= tr;
                return tr;
            }
        }

        return 0;
    }

    int maxflow(int s, int t) {
        int flow = 0;
        while (bfs(s, t)) {
            fill(iter.begin(), iter.end(), 0);
            while (int pushed = dfs(s, t, INF)) {
                flow += pushed;
            }
        }
        return flow;
    }

    vector<bool> getSourceSide(int s) {
        vector<bool> reachable(n, false);
        queue<int> q;
        q.push(s);
        reachable[s] = true;

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int v = q.front();
            q.pop();
            for (int id : g[v]) {
                int to = edges[id].to;
                if (!reachable[to] && edges[id].flow < edges[id].cap) {
                    reachable[to] = true;
                    q.push(to);
                }
            }
        }

        return reachable;
    }
};

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;

    vector<pair<int, int>> edges(m);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        edges[i] = {u, v};
    }

    if (n <= 1) {
        cout << 0 << "\n";
        return 0;
    }

    int minCut = INF;
    int bestTarget = 1;

    for (int t = 1; t < n; t++) {
        MaxFlow mf(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
            mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
        }

        int flow = mf.maxflow(0, t);
        if (flow < minCut) {
            minCut = flow;
            bestTarget = t;
        }

        if (minCut == 1) break;
    }

    MaxFlow mf(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].first, edges[i].second, 1);
        mf.addEdge(edges[i].second, edges[i].first, 1);
    }

    mf.maxflow(0, bestTarget);

    vector<bool> sourceSide = mf.getSourceSide(0);

    vector<pair<int, int>> cutEdges;
    for (auto [u, v] : edges) {
        if (sourceSide[u] && !sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({u, v});
        } else if (!sourceSide[u] && sourceSide[v]) {
            cutEdges.push_back({v, u});
        }
    }

    cout << minCut << "\n";
    for (auto [u, v] : cutEdges) {
        cout << u << " " << v << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 19: 3D Voxel Mining (C#) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a C# program to solve a mining problem in a simplified 3D voxel space.

**Constraints:**
1. Can only dig dirt that is exposed from above or a side.
2. Dug dirt must be dumped somewhere - at a location not blocking future digging
3. Maximum uphill slope - 1 Z voxel per X/Y voxel.
4. Dumped dirt becomes a new obstacle (plan carefully!)
5. Goal is to get valuable ore to a processing plant located at x=0, y=0
6. Ground is stable and solid, and you can dig under any voxel without fear of it collapsing
so long as it's connected to the earth. If you disconnect a voxel(s), it will collapse.

The voxel grid size has 10x more precision in Z than X/Y. So 10x10x100 is a cube.

**Input header format (stdin - space delimited. Ends with newline):**
```
XVoxelCount YVoxelCount ZVoxelCount XBlockSize YBlockSize ZBlockSize
```
Counts are human readable integers. BlockSize are human readable decimal numbers.

After the newline, voxels then follow in bytes, like this python psuedocode:
```
for x in range(XVoxelCount):
for y in range(YVoxelCount):
for z in range(ZVoxelCount):
d = readByte(stdin)
massInTonnes = d % 16
valuePerTonne = d // 16
addVoxel(d)
```

Each peice of dirt needs to be moved to its new location - you need to trace the
path in X and Y (4-connected / manhatten steps) and ensure that the path doesn't climb uphill
or downhill more than 1 Z voxel for every X/Y voxel.

**Output format (stdout / newline seperated):**
```
M (number of operations)
N digX digY digZ stepX1 stepY1 stepX2 stepY2 (N steps) destX destY (for each operation)
```

If dumping valuable ore at the processing plant, the voxel ceases to exist. If a voxel is
dumped elsewhere, it is dumped on top of the ground, raising the ground by 1 voxel. It can
be dug again and moved later if required.

Every unit of mass requires $1 of fuel to move one voxel in X or Y when flat, or $5 of fuel
to go up a 10% slope. Downhill is free.

Getting valuable ore to your processing plant pays $1000 per unit of mass per unit of value.
The most valuable ore (#15) pays $15,000 per ton, so the most a single dump truck load can gross
is $225,000.

(Dumping junk ore at the processing plant causes damage to the plant, costing $1000 per ton.)

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with .NET/csc
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. World size could be anything from 10x10x10 to 5000x5000x5000, your code should handle both extremes.
4. Your code should complete in under 5 minutes
5. Maximize profit.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (.NET SDK 10.0.201; SDKs 10.0.201) | csc 5.3.0-2.26153.122 (4d3023de605a78ba3e59e50c657eed70f125c68a) | max LangVersion 14


Write complete, compilable C# code with a Main method.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

View chain of thought

Plan an open-pit mine in a 3D block model. Each block has a value (ore worth money, waste costs money to move) and you can only dig a block once the blocks above it have been removed. The goal is to maximise profit by choosing which blocks to extract and where to dump the waste.

The AI must balance revenue against haulage costs, respect slope constraints for road access, and avoid dumping waste where it blocks future digging. Subpasses increase the pit size and complexity.

3D Voxel Mining - 10×10 tiny pit (10 KB)

View C# Code (5993 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.IO;

class MiningOptimizer
{
    private int xCount, yCount, zCount;
    private double xBlockSize, yBlockSize, zBlockSize;
    private byte[,,] voxels;
    private bool[,,] dug;

    static void Main()
    {
        var optimizer = new MiningOptimizer();
        optimizer.Run();
    }

    void Run()
    {
        ReadInput();
        var operations = GenerateOperations();
        WriteOutput(operations);
    }

    void ReadInput()
    {
        var header = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        xCount = int.Parse(header[0]);
        yCount = int.Parse(header[1]);
        zCount = int.Parse(header[2]);
        xBlockSize = double.Parse(header[3]);
        yBlockSize = double.Parse(header[4]);
        zBlockSize = double.Parse(header[5]);

        voxels = new byte[xCount, yCount, zCount];
        dug = new bool[xCount, yCount, zCount];

        var stdin = Console.OpenStandardInput();
        int totalBytes = xCount * yCount * zCount;
        byte[] buffer = new byte[totalBytes];
        stdin.Read(buffer, 0, totalBytes);

        int idx = 0;
        for (int x = 0; x < xCount; x++)
        {
            for (int y = 0; y < yCount; y++)
            {
                for (int z = 0; z < zCount; z++)
                {
                    voxels[x, y, z] = buffer[idx++];
                }
            }
        }
    }

    List<string> GenerateOperations()
    {
        var operations = new List<string>();

        // Collect all ore with value > 0
        var oreVoxels = new List<(int x, int y, int z, int mass, int value, double profitScore)>();

        for (int x = 0; x < xCount; x++)
        {
            for (int y = 0; y < yCount; y++)
            {
                for (int z = 0; z < zCount; z++)
                {
                    if (dug[x, y, z]) continue;

                    byte voxel = voxels[x, y, z];
                    int mass = voxel % 16;
                    int value = voxel / 16;

                    if (value > 0 && mass > 0)
                    {
                        // Profit score: (mass * value * 1000) / (transport_cost_estimate)
                        // Transport cost estimate: manhattan distance * cost_per_step
                        int manDist = Math.Abs(x) + Math.Abs(y);
                        double costEst = Math.Max(1, manDist); // Avoid division by zero
                        double profitScore = (mass * value * 1000.0) / costEst;

                        oreVoxels.Add((x, y, z, mass, value, profitScore));
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // Sort by profit score (descending)
        oreVoxels.Sort((a, b) => b.profitScore.CompareTo(a.profitScore));

        // Process highest profit ore first
        foreach (var (x, y, z, mass, value, profitScore) in oreVoxels)
        {
            if (dug[x, y, z]) continue; // Already processed

            // Check if ore is exposed (at surface level or has empty space nearby)
            if (IsExposed(x, y, z))
            {
                // Calculate actual movement cost
                int manDist = Math.Abs(x) + Math.Abs(y);
                double revenue = mass * value * 1000.0;
                double cost = mass * manDist; // Simplified: $1 per unit mass per step

                // Only move if profitable
                if (revenue > cost * 1.5) // 50% profit margin threshold
                {
                    var path = GeneratePath(x, y, 0, 0);
                    if (path.Count > 0)
                    {
                        var operation = FormatOperation(x, y, z, path);
                        if (operation != null)
                        {
                            operations.Add(operation);
                            dug[x, y, z] = true;
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        return operations;
    }

    bool IsExposed(int x, int y, int z)
    {
        // Check if voxel is at the surface (top of stack at this x,y)
        // or has empty space (dug area) adjacent

        // Check above
        if (z == zCount - 1) return true;
        if (dug[x, y, z + 1]) return true;

        // Check sides (4-connected)
        int[] dx = { 1, -1, 0, 0 };
        int[] dy = { 0, 0, 1, -1 };

        for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++)
        {
            int nx = x + dx[i];
            int ny = y + dy[i];

            if (nx >= 0 && nx < xCount && ny >= 0 && ny < yCount)
            {
                if (dug[nx, ny, z])
                    return true;
            }
            else
            {
                // Outside boundary = exposed to the edge
                return true;
            }
        }

        return false;
    }

    List<(int x, int y)> GeneratePath(int startX, int startY, int endX, int endY)
    {
        var path = new List<(int, int)>();

        int x = startX, y = startY;

        // Generate manhattan path to destination
        while (x != endX || y != endY)
        {
            if (x > endX) x--;
            else if (x < endX) x++;
            else if (y > endY) y--;
            else if (y < endY) y++;

            path.Add((x, y));
        }

        return path;
    }

    string FormatOperation(int digX, int digY, int digZ, List<(int x, int y)> path)
    {
        if (path.Count == 0) return null;

        var sb = new System.Text.StringBuilder();
        sb.Append($"{path.Count} {digX} {digY} {digZ}");

        // Add all path steps
        foreach (var (x, y) in path)
        {
            sb.Append($" {x} {y}");
        }

        // Add destination (last position in path)
        var lastPos = path[path.Count - 1];
        sb.Append($" {lastPos.x} {lastPos.y}");

        return sb.ToString();
    }

    void WriteOutput(List<string> operations)
    {
        Console.WriteLine(operations.Count);
        foreach (var op in operations)
        {
            Console.WriteLine(op);
        }
    }
}

Test 20: Drillhole Data Validation (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a Rust program to validate drillhole assay data.


**The Challenge:**
Your Rust program will be tested with drillhole datasets ranging from small exploration projects to
massive mining surveys. The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales from 10 holes to
200,000 holes.

**Problem:**
Given drillhole data from a mining exploration project, identify samples that likely contain typos or
measurement errors. These outliers don't correlate well with spatially neighboring data points.

**Target environment specs**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler information**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

**Drillhole Data:**
- Each hole has a 3D path: start point (x,y,z) + direction vector + length
- Samples are taken at intervals along each hole
- Each sample has measurements for multiple properties

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_holes num_properties
property_name_1 property_name_2 ...
hole_id start_x start_y start_z dir_x dir_y dir_z length num_samples
depth prop1 prop2 ...
depth prop1 prop2 ...
etc.
hole_id start_x start_y start_z dir_x dir_y dir_z length num_samples
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
M (number of suspect entries found)
hole_id sample_idx property_name confidence (for each suspect, sorted by confidence desc)
```

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with rustc and only the standard rust library.
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle up to thousands of holes with thousands of samples each
4. Complete within 5 minutes
5. Report at least the most obvious outliers
6. Confidence score should reflect how anomalous the value is
7. Must handle varying dataset sizes efficiently

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on dataset scale.

The program should be robust enough to handle edge cases like missing data, duplicate entries, and varying data distributions.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

View chain of thought

Given a set of mining drillholes — each a 3D path with measurements taken at intervals along its length — identify the data entries that are most likely to be errors (typos, instrument glitches, or transcription mistakes).

The AI must compare each measurement against its spatial neighbours: a reading that disagrees with nearby drillholes is suspect. Subpasses increase the number of drillholes and properties, requiring efficient spatial reasoning at scale.

Test 21: N-Dimensional Snake Game (C++) Score: 3.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C++ code to play an N-Dimensional Snake Game.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY dimensional complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2D games (eg 8x8), few obstacles, basic pathfinding
- **Medium**: 3D-4D games (eg 6x6x6 to 8x8x8x8), moderate obstacles, intermediate planning
- **Large**: 6D-8D games (eg 4x4x4x4x4x4 to 6x6x6x6x6x6x6x6), many obstacles, complex pathfinding
- **Extreme**: 10D-20D games (eg 3x3x3... to 4x4x4...)

**The Challenge:**
Your C++ snake player will be tested with games ranging from simple 2D boards to massive 20D hypercubes.
The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL dimensionalities.

**Problem:**
Like classic Nokia snake, but generalized to arbitrary dimensions. The snake occupies a series of
connected integer grid points in N-D space, moves one step per turn along one axis (+1 or -1),
grows when eating food, and dies if it hits an obstacle or itself.

**Input format (stdin, whitespace / newline seperated):**
```
D max_turns (D = number of dimensions, max_turns = turn limit)
bounds (D integers: size of space in each dimension)
snake_length start_pos (length, then D integers for head position)
F (number of food items)
F lines: food positions (D integers each)
O (number of obstacles)
O lines: obstacle positions (D integers each)
```

**Output format (stdout):**
Each turn, output: axis direction
- axis: 0 to D-1 (which dimension to move along)
- direction: -1 or +1 (which way to move)

**Game ends when:**
- Snake eats all food (win)
- Snake hits obstacle or itself (lose)
- Max turns reached

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Play the classic Snake game, but in more than two dimensions. The snake moves through an N-dimensional grid, eating food to grow while avoiding its own body and the walls. In higher dimensions the grid is enormous but self-collision becomes far trickier to reason about.

Subpasses increase the number of dimensions, making the state space explode. The AI must write a strategy that plans a safe path to food without boxing itself in.

 1.0000
[2D 20x20, 5 food, 10 obstacles] Food: 5/5, Turns: 31, End: won, Time: 0.15s

N-Dimensional Snake - 2D 20x20, 5 food, 10 obstacles

View C++ Code (4965 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <set>
#include <cmath>

using namespace std;

int main() {
    int D, max_turns;
    cin >> D >> max_turns;

    vector<int> bounds(D);
    for (int i = 0; i < D; i++) {
        cin >> bounds[i];
    }

    int initial_length;
    cin >> initial_length;

    vector<int> head(D);
    for (int i = 0; i < D; i++) {
        cin >> head[i];
    }

    vector<vector<int>> snake;
    snake.push_back(head);

    int F;
    cin >> F;
    vector<vector<int>> food(F);
    for (int i = 0; i < F; i++) {
        food[i].resize(D);
        for (int j = 0; j < D; j++) {
            cin >> food[i][j];
        }
    }

    int O;
    cin >> O;
    set<vector<int>> obstacles;
    for (int i = 0; i < O; i++) {
        vector<int> obs(D);
        for (int j = 0; j < D; j++) {
            cin >> obs[j];
        }
        obstacles.insert(obs);
    }

    int current_length = initial_length;

    for (int turn = 0; turn < max_turns && !food.empty(); turn++) {
        // Find nearest food using Manhattan distance
        int target_idx = 0;
        double min_dist = 1e18;

        for (size_t i = 0; i < food.size(); i++) {
            double dist = 0;
            for (int j = 0; j < D; j++) {
                dist += abs(head[j] - food[i][j]);
            }
            if (dist < min_dist) {
                min_dist = dist;
                target_idx = i;
            }
        }

        vector<int> target = food[target_idx];

        // Find best valid move (greedy: closest to target food)
        int best_axis = 0;
        int best_dir = 1;
        double best_dist = 1e18;
        bool found_move = false;

        for (int axis = 0; axis < D; axis++) {
            for (int dir : {-1, 1}) {
                vector<int> new_head = head;
                new_head[axis] += dir;

                // Check bounds
                bool valid = true;
                for (int j = 0; j < D; j++) {
                    if (new_head[j] < 0 || new_head[j] >= bounds[j]) {
                        valid = false;
                        break;
                    }
                }
                if (!valid) continue;

                // Check obstacles
                if (obstacles.count(new_head)) continue;

                // Check self collision (all body except tail which moves away)
                bool collision = false;
                for (size_t i = 0; i < snake.size() - 1; i++) {
                    if (snake[i] == new_head) {
                        collision = true;
                        break;
                    }
                }
                if (collision) continue;

                found_move = true;

                // Distance to target food
                double dist = 0;
                for (int j = 0; j < D; j++) {
                    dist += abs(new_head[j] - target[j]);
                }

                if (dist < best_dist) {
                    best_dist = dist;
                    best_axis = axis;
                    best_dir = dir;
                }
            }
        }

        if (!found_move) {
            // No move towards food found, try any valid move
            for (int axis = 0; axis < D && !found_move; axis++) {
                for (int dir : {-1, 1}) {
                    vector<int> new_head = head;
                    new_head[axis] += dir;

                    bool valid = true;
                    for (int j = 0; j < D; j++) {
                        if (new_head[j] < 0 || new_head[j] >= bounds[j]) {
                            valid = false;
                            break;
                        }
                    }
                    if (!valid) continue;

                    if (obstacles.count(new_head)) continue;

                    bool collision = false;
                    for (size_t i = 0; i < snake.size() - 1; i++) {
                        if (snake[i] == new_head) {
                            collision = true;
                            break;
                        }
                    }
                    if (collision) continue;

                    best_axis = axis;
                    best_dir = dir;
                    found_move = true;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }

        if (!found_move) {
            break;
        }

        // Execute move
        vector<int> new_head = head;
        new_head[best_axis] += best_dir;

        snake.insert(snake.begin(), new_head);
        head = new_head;

        // Check if food was eaten
        for (size_t i = 0; i < food.size(); i++) {
            if (food[i] == head) {
                food.erase(food.begin() + i);
                current_length++;
                break;
            }
        }

        // Remove tail if not growing
        if ((int)snake.size() > current_length) {
            snake.pop_back();
        }

        cout << best_axis << " " << best_dir << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}
