3D Point Clustering - 10K points, 10 clusters

View Rust Code (8000 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufReader, BufWriter, Read, Write};

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct Point {
    x: f32,
    y: f32,
    z: f32,
}

impl Point {
    fn distance_squared(&self, other: &Point) -> f32 {
        let dx = self.x - other.x;
        let dy = self.y - other.y;
        let dz = self.z - other.z;
        dx * dx + dy * dy + dz * dz
    }

    fn from_bytes(bytes: &[u8]) -> Self {
        let x = f32::from_le_bytes([bytes[0], bytes[1], bytes[2], bytes[3]]);
        let y = f32::from_le_bytes([bytes[4], bytes[5], bytes[6], bytes[7]]);
        let z = f32::from_le_bytes([bytes[8], bytes[9], bytes[10], bytes[11]]);
        Point { x, y, z }
    }
}

struct Centroid {
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
    z: f64,
    count: u64,
}

impl Centroid {
    fn new(p: &Point) -> Self {
        Centroid {
            x: p.x as f64,
            y: p.y as f64,
            z: p.z as f64,
            count: 1,
        }
    }

    fn add(&mut self, p: &Point) {
        self.x += p.x as f64;
        self.y += p.y as f64;
        self.z += p.z as f64;
        self.count += 1;
    }

    fn to_point(&self) -> Point {
        if self.count == 0 {
            Point { x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0 }
        } else {
            let c = self.count as f64;
            Point {
                x: (self.x / c) as f32,
                y: (self.y / c) as f32,
                z: (self.z / c) as f32,
            }
        }
    }

    fn reset(&mut self) {
        self.x = 0.0;
        self.y = 0.0;
        self.z = 0.0;
        self.count = 0;
    }
}

fn find_nearest_centroid(point: &Point, centroids: &[Point]) -> usize {
    let mut best_idx = 0;
    let mut best_dist = f32::INFINITY;
    for (i, centroid) in centroids.iter().enumerate() {
        let dist = point.distance_squared(centroid);
        if dist < best_dist {
            best_dist = dist;
            best_idx = i;
        }
    }
    best_idx
}

fn initialize_centroids(k: usize, first_points: &[Point]) -> Vec<Point> {
    let mut centroids = Vec::new();

    if k == 0 {
        return centroids;
    }

    if first_points.is_empty() {
        for i in 0..k {
            let angle = (i as f32) * 2.0 * std::f32::consts::PI / (k as f32);
            centroids.push(Point {
                x: angle.cos(),
                y: angle.sin(),
                z: 0.0,
            });
        }
        return centroids;
    }

    centroids.push(first_points[0].clone());
    let mut rng = 12345u64;

    while centroids.len() < k {
        rng = rng.wrapping_mul(1103515245).wrapping_add(12345);
        let idx = (rng as usize) % first_points.len();
        let candidate = &first_points[idx];

        let min_dist = centroids
            .iter()
            .map(|c| candidate.distance_squared(c))
            .fold(f32::INFINITY, f32::min);

        if min_dist > 1e-6 {
            centroids.push(candidate.clone());
        } else if centroids.len() < k {
            let angle = (centroids.len() as f32) * 2.0 * std::f32::consts::PI / (k as f32);
            centroids.push(Point {
                x: angle.cos() + candidate.x,
                y: angle.sin() + candidate.y,
                z: candidate.z,
            });
        }
    }

    centroids.truncate(k);
    centroids
}

fn main() -> io::Result<()> {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut input = BufReader::new(stdin.lock());

    let mut header = [0u8; 8];
    input.read_exact(&mut header)?;

    let num_points = u32::from_le_bytes([header[0], header[1], header[2], header[3]]) as usize;
    let num_clusters = u32::from_le_bytes([header[4], header[5], header[6], header[7]]) as usize;

    if num_clusters == 0 || num_points == 0 {
        return Ok(());
    }

    let stdout = io::stdout();
    let mut output = BufWriter::new(stdout.lock());

    let mut cluster_assignments = vec![0u32; num_points];

    let sample_size = (100_000).min(num_points);
    let mut initial_points = Vec::new();
    let mut buffer = vec![0u8; 12 * 1024];

    let step = if num_points > sample_size {
        num_points / sample_size
    } else {
        1
    };

    let mut sampled = 0;
    let mut total_read = 0;

    loop {
        match input.read(&mut buffer)? {
            0 => break,
            bytes_read => {
                for chunk in buffer[..bytes_read].chunks(12) {
                    if chunk.len() == 12 {
                        if sampled % step == 0 && initial_points.len() < sample_size {
                            initial_points.push(Point::from_bytes(chunk));
                        }
                        sampled += 1;
                        total_read += 1;
                    }
                }
                if initial_points.len() >= sample_size {
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    }

    let mut centroids = initialize_centroids(num_clusters, &initial_points);

    let batch_size = if num_points <= 10_000_000 {
        1_000_000
    } else if num_points <= 100_000_000 {
        500_000
    } else {
        100_000
    };

    let max_iterations = if num_points <= 10_000_000 {
        20
    } else if num_points <= 100_000_000 {
        10
    } else {
        5
    };

    for iteration in 0..max_iterations {
        let mut centroid_accum: Vec<Centroid> = Vec::new();
        for c in &centroids {
            let mut acc = Centroid::new(c);
            acc.reset();
            centroid_accum.push(acc);
        }

        let stdin = io::stdin();
        let mut input = BufReader::new(stdin.lock());
        let mut buffer = vec![0u8; 12 * batch_size];

        let mut point_idx = 0;
        loop {
            match input.read(&mut buffer)? {
                0 => break,
                bytes_read => {
                    for chunk in buffer[..bytes_read].chunks(12) {
                        if chunk.len() == 12 && point_idx < num_points {
                            let point = Point::from_bytes(chunk);
                            let nearest = find_nearest_centroid(&point, &centroids);
                            centroid_accum[nearest].add(&point);
                            cluster_assignments[point_idx] = nearest as u32;
                            point_idx += 1;
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        let old_centroids = centroids.clone();
        centroids.clear();

        for (i, acc) in centroid_accum.iter().enumerate() {
            if acc.count > 0 {
                centroids.push(acc.to_point());
            } else {
                if i < old_centroids.len() {
                    centroids.push(old_centroids[i].clone());
                } else {
                    centroids.push(Point { x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0 });
                }
            }
        }

        let mut converged = true;
        if iteration > 1 {
            for (old, new) in old_centroids.iter().zip(centroids.iter()) {
                if old.distance_squared(new) > 1e-5 {
                    converged = false;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }

        if converged && iteration > 2 {
            break;
        }
    }

    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut input = BufReader::new(stdin.lock());
    let mut buffer = vec![0u8; 12 * batch_size];
    let mut point_idx = 0;

    loop {
        match input.read(&mut buffer)? {
            0 => break,
            bytes_read => {
                for chunk in buffer[..bytes_read].chunks(12) {
                    if chunk.len() == 12 && point_idx < num_points {
                        let point = Point::from_bytes(chunk);
                        let nearest = find_nearest_centroid(&point, &centroids);
                        cluster_assignments[point_idx] = nearest as u32;
                        point_idx += 1;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }

    for &cluster_id in &cluster_assignments {
        output.write_all(&cluster_id.to_le_bytes())?;
    }
    output.flush()?;

    Ok(())
}

View Rust Code (8000 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufReader, BufWriter, Read, Write};

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
struct Point {
    x: f32,
    y: f32,
    z: f32,
}

impl Point {
    fn distance_squared(&self, other: &Point) -> f32 {
        let dx = self.x - other.x;
        let dy = self.y - other.y;
        let dz = self.z - other.z;
        dx * dx + dy * dy + dz * dz
    }

    fn from_bytes(bytes: &[u8]) -> Self {
        let x = f32::from_le_bytes([bytes[0], bytes[1], bytes[2], bytes[3]]);
        let y = f32::from_le_bytes([bytes[4], bytes[5], bytes[6], bytes[7]]);
        let z = f32::from_le_bytes([bytes[8], bytes[9], bytes[10], bytes[11]]);
        Point { x, y, z }
    }
}

struct Centroid {
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
    z: f64,
    count: u64,
}

impl Centroid {
    fn new(p: &Point) -> Self {
        Centroid {
            x: p.x as f64,
            y: p.y as f64,
            z: p.z as f64,
            count: 1,
        }
    }

    fn add(&mut self, p: &Point) {
        self.x += p.x as f64;
        self.y += p.y as f64;
        self.z += p.z as f64;
        self.count += 1;
    }

    fn to_point(&self) -> Point {
        if self.count == 0 {
            Point { x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0 }
        } else {
            let c = self.count as f64;
            Point {
                x: (self.x / c) as f32,
                y: (self.y / c) as f32,
                z: (self.z / c) as f32,
            }
        }
    }

    fn reset(&mut self) {
        self.x = 0.0;
        self.y = 0.0;
        self.z = 0.0;
        self.count = 0;
    }
}

fn find_nearest_centroid(point: &Point, centroids: &[Point]) -> usize {
    let mut best_idx = 0;
    let mut best_dist = f32::INFINITY;
    for (i, centroid) in centroids.iter().enumerate() {
        let dist = point.distance_squared(centroid);
        if dist < best_dist {
            best_dist = dist;
            best_idx = i;
        }
    }
    best_idx
}

fn initialize_centroids(k: usize, first_points: &[Point]) -> Vec<Point> {
    let mut centroids = Vec::new();

    if k == 0 {
        return centroids;
    }

    if first_points.is_empty() {
        for i in 0..k {
            let angle = (i as f32) * 2.0 * std::f32::consts::PI / (k as f32);
            centroids.push(Point {
                x: angle.cos(),
                y: angle.sin(),
                z: 0.0,
            });
        }
        return centroids;
    }

    centroids.push(first_points[0].clone());
    let mut rng = 12345u64;

    while centroids.len() < k {
        rng = rng.wrapping_mul(1103515245).wrapping_add(12345);
        let idx = (rng as usize) % first_points.len();
        let candidate = &first_points[idx];

        let min_dist = centroids
            .iter()
            .map(|c| candidate.distance_squared(c))
            .fold(f32::INFINITY, f32::min);

        if min_dist > 1e-6 {
            centroids.push(candidate.clone());
        } else if centroids.len() < k {
            let angle = (centroids.len() as f32) * 2.0 * std::f32::consts::PI / (k as f32);
            centroids.push(Point {
                x: angle.cos() + candidate.x,
                y: angle.sin() + candidate.y,
                z: candidate.z,
            });
        }
    }

    centroids.truncate(k);
    centroids
}

fn main() -> io::Result<()> {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut input = BufReader::new(stdin.lock());

    let mut header = [0u8; 8];
    input.read_exact(&mut header)?;

    let num_points = u32::from_le_bytes([header[0], header[1], header[2], header[3]]) as usize;
    let num_clusters = u32::from_le_bytes([header[4], header[5], header[6], header[7]]) as usize;

    if num_clusters == 0 || num_points == 0 {
        return Ok(());
    }

    let stdout = io::stdout();
    let mut output = BufWriter::new(stdout.lock());

    let mut cluster_assignments = vec![0u32; num_points];

    let sample_size = (100_000).min(num_points);
    let mut initial_points = Vec::new();
    let mut buffer = vec![0u8; 12 * 1024];

    let step = if num_points > sample_size {
        num_points / sample_size
    } else {
        1
    };

    let mut sampled = 0;
    let mut total_read = 0;

    loop {
        match input.read(&mut buffer)? {
            0 => break,
            bytes_read => {
                for chunk in buffer[..bytes_read].chunks(12) {
                    if chunk.len() == 12 {
                        if sampled % step == 0 && initial_points.len() < sample_size {
                            initial_points.push(Point::from_bytes(chunk));
                        }
                        sampled += 1;
                        total_read += 1;
                    }
                }
                if initial_points.len() >= sample_size {
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    }

    let mut centroids = initialize_centroids(num_clusters, &initial_points);

    let batch_size = if num_points <= 10_000_000 {
        1_000_000
    } else if num_points <= 100_000_000 {
        500_000
    } else {
        100_000
    };

    let max_iterations = if num_points <= 10_000_000 {
        20
    } else if num_points <= 100_000_000 {
        10
    } else {
        5
    };

    for iteration in 0..max_iterations {
        let mut centroid_accum: Vec<Centroid> = Vec::new();
        for c in &centroids {
            let mut acc = Centroid::new(c);
            acc.reset();
            centroid_accum.push(acc);
        }

        let stdin = io::stdin();
        let mut input = BufReader::new(stdin.lock());
        let mut buffer = vec![0u8; 12 * batch_size];

        let mut point_idx = 0;
        loop {
            match input.read(&mut buffer)? {
                0 => break,
                bytes_read => {
                    for chunk in buffer[..bytes_read].chunks(12) {
                        if chunk.len() == 12 && point_idx < num_points {
                            let point = Point::from_bytes(chunk);
                            let nearest = find_nearest_centroid(&point, &centroids);
                            centroid_accum[nearest].add(&point);
                            cluster_assignments[point_idx] = nearest as u32;
                            point_idx += 1;
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        let old_centroids = centroids.clone();
        centroids.clear();

        for (i, acc) in centroid_accum.iter().enumerate() {
            if acc.count > 0 {
                centroids.push(acc.to_point());
            } else {
                if i < old_centroids.len() {
                    centroids.push(old_centroids[i].clone());
                } else {
                    centroids.push(Point { x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0 });
                }
            }
        }

        let mut converged = true;
        if iteration > 1 {
            for (old, new) in old_centroids.iter().zip(centroids.iter()) {
                if old.distance_squared(new) > 1e-5 {
                    converged = false;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }

        if converged && iteration > 2 {
            break;
        }
    }

    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut input = BufReader::new(stdin.lock());
    let mut buffer = vec![0u8; 12 * batch_size];
    let mut point_idx = 0;

    loop {
        match input.read(&mut buffer)? {
            0 => break,
            bytes_read => {
                for chunk in buffer[..bytes_read].chunks(12) {
                    if chunk.len() == 12 && point_idx < num_points {
                        let point = Point::from_bytes(chunk);
                        let nearest = find_nearest_centroid(&point, &centroids);
                        cluster_assignments[point_idx] = nearest as u32;
                        point_idx += 1;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }

    for &cluster_id in &cluster_assignments {
        output.write_all(&cluster_id.to_le_bytes())?;
    }
    output.flush()?;

    Ok(())
}

Test 27: Graph Coloring (C++) Score: 0.00 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C++ code to solve the Graph Coloring problem.

**The Challenge:**
Your C++ graph colorer will be tested with graphs ranging from simple 50 nodes
to massive million-node graphs requiring 60 colours.

**Problem:**
Color a graph with exactly k colours such that no two adjacent vertices share the same colour.
This is NP-Complete and requires sophisticated algorithms for larger instances.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_vertices num_edges num_colors
u v (for each edge, 0-indexed)
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
color_0 color_1 ... color_(n-1) (color assignment for each vertex)
```

**Success Criteria:**
- All vertices assigned valid colors (0 to k-1)
- No adjacent vertices share the same color
- All vertices are colored (complete coloring)

**Failure Criteria:**
- Cannot find valid coloring within time limit
- Invalid color assignment (adjacent vertices same color)
- Incomplete coloring

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with g++ or MSVC (C++17)
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle variable graph sizes and color counts
4. Complete within 5 minutes
5. Must handle varying graph complexities efficiently

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on graph complexity.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Colour the nodes of a graph using as few colours as possible, with the rule that no two connected nodes may share the same colour. This is the same idea as colouring a map so that no neighbouring countries are the same colour.

Finding the minimum number of colours is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the graph size and connectivity. The AI’s colouring must be valid (no clashes) and use as few colours as possible.

 0.0000
[20 vertices, 4 colors] Invalid color -1, must be in [0, 3]

Graph Coloring - 20 vertices, 4 colors

View C++ Code (2258 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int num_vertices, num_edges, num_colors;
    cin >> num_vertices >> num_edges >> num_colors;

    // Build adjacency list
    vector<vector<int>> adj(num_vertices);
    for (int i = 0; i < num_edges; i++) {
        int u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        adj[u].push_back(v);
        adj[v].push_back(u);
    }

    vector<int> color(num_vertices, -1);
    vector<bool> usedColors(num_colors);

    // Adaptive strategy: Welsh-Powell for medium graphs, simple greedy for very large
    if (num_vertices <= 100000) {
        // Welsh-Powell: Order vertices by degree (descending) for better heuristic
        vector<int> order(num_vertices);
        for (int i = 0; i < num_vertices; i++) {
            order[i] = i;
        }

        // Sort by degree (descending)
        sort(order.begin(), order.end(), [&](int a, int b) {
            return adj[a].size() > adj[b].size();
        });

        // Greedy coloring with sorted order
        for (int v : order) {
            fill(usedColors.begin(), usedColors.end(), false);
            for (int u : adj[v]) {
                if (color[u] != -1) {
                    usedColors[color[u]] = true;
                }
            }

            for (int c = 0; c < num_colors; c++) {
                if (!usedColors[c]) {
                    color[v] = c;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    } else {
        // Simple greedy for very large graphs (faster, no sorting overhead)
        for (int v = 0; v < num_vertices; v++) {
            fill(usedColors.begin(), usedColors.end(), false);
            for (int u : adj[v]) {
                if (color[u] != -1) {
                    usedColors[color[u]] = true;
                }
            }

            for (int c = 0; c < num_colors; c++) {
                if (!usedColors[c]) {
                    color[v] = c;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Output coloring
    for (int i = 0; i < num_vertices; i++) {
        if (i > 0) cout << " ";
        cout << color[i];
    }
    cout << "\n";

    return 0;
}

Test 28: Minimum Vertex Cover (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 17
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Rust code to solve the Minimum Vertex Cover problem.

Your code may see graphs with up to 2M vertices and 15M edges.

**Problem:**
Find the minimum set of vertices such that every edge has at least one endpoint in the set.
This is NP-Hard and requires sophisticated algorithms for larger instances.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_vertices num_edges
u v (for each edge, 0-indexed)
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
vertex_count
vertex_1 vertex_2 ... vertex_k (vertices in the cover, space-separated)
```

**Success Criteria:**
- All edges are covered by selected vertices
- Vertex set size is minimized (or near-minimized for large graphs)
- Output format is correct

**Failure Criteria:**
- Not all edges are covered
- Timeout without solution
- Invalid output format

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Be aware that default warnings are enabled and will cause a compilation failure,
so ensure that you write warning-free code.

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.

View chain of thought

Find the smallest set of nodes in a graph such that every edge has at least one endpoint in the set. Imagine placing guards at intersections so that every road is watched by at least one guard — use as few guards as possible.

This is a classic NP-hard problem. The test uses planted instances with a known optimal answer, so grading is strict pass/fail. Subpasses increase the graph size.

 0.0000
Compilation error: Compilation failed:
error[E0599]: no method named `lines` found for struct `StdinLock<'a>` in the current scope
--> C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpep16p1j5.rs:6:34
|
6 | let mut lines = std

Minimum Vertex Cover - 10 vertices, 30 edges

View Rust Code (1258 chars)
use std::collections::HashSet;
use std::io;

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read the first line
    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let _num_vertices: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let num_edges: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    // Store edges
    let mut edges = Vec::new();
    for _ in 0..num_edges {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
        let u: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
        let v: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
        edges.push((u, v));
    }

    // Greedy vertex cover: for each uncovered edge, add both endpoints
    let mut cover = HashSet::new();

    for (u, v) in edges {
        if !cover.contains(&u) && !cover.contains(&v) {
            cover.insert(u);
            cover.insert(v);
        }
    }

    // Output
    let mut result: Vec<usize> = cover.into_iter().collect();
    result.sort();

    println!("{}", result.len());
    let output = result.iter()
        .map(|v| v.to_string())
        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
        .join(" ");
    println!("{}", output);
}

Test 29: Maximum Clique (C#) Score: 0.00 / 18
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C# code to solve the Maximum Clique problem, which is NP Hard.

Your C# maximum clique finder will be tested with graphs ranging from sparse to very dense instances.
The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL graph complexities, from 20 vertices to 1 million.

**Problem:**
Find the largest complete subgraph (clique) in an undirected graph.
A clique is a set of vertices where every pair of vertices is connected by an edge.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_vertices num_edges
u v (for each edge, 0-indexed)
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
clique_size
vertex_1 vertex_2 ... vertex_k (vertices in the maximum clique, space-separated)
```

**Success Criteria:**
- All vertices in output form a complete subgraph
- No larger clique exists in the graph
- Output format is correct

**Failure Criteria:**
- Output vertices don't form a clique
- Larger clique exists
- Timeout without solution
- Invalid output format

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with .NET/csc
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle variable graph sizes and densities
4. Complete within 5 minutes
5. Must handle varying graph complexities efficiently

Hints:
- You may wish to drop to a hueristic if time is running out for partial score.
- Use threads to process in parallel.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (.NET SDK 10.0.201; SDKs 10.0.201) | csc 5.3.0-2.26153.122 (4d3023de605a78ba3e59e50c657eed70f125c68a) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a Main method.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find the largest group of nodes in a graph where every pair is directly connected. Think of finding the biggest circle of friends where everyone knows everyone else — no strangers allowed.

This is one of the hardest problems in computer science (NP-hard). The test uses planted instances with a known answer for strict pass/fail grading. Subpasses increase the graph size and density.

 0.0000
Compilation error: Compilation failed:
Determining projects to restore...
Restored D:\CodingBenchmark\compile_cache\claude-haiku-4-5-LowReasoning\csharp\project_b1a9aa5bf883bc26\Program.csproj (in 181 ms).
CSC : err

Maximum Clique - 20 vertices, dense

View C# Code (2539 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        var line = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        int n = int.Parse(line[0]);
        int m = int.Parse(line[1]);

        var adj = new List<HashSet<int>>(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            adj.Add(new HashSet<int>());

        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++)
        {
            var parts = Console.ReadLine().Split();
            int u = int.Parse(parts[0]);
            int v = int.Parse(parts[1]);
            adj[u].Add(v);
            adj[v].Add(u);
        }

        var cts = new CancellationTokenSource(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(270));
        List<int> bestClique = new();
        object lockObj = new();

        int numStarts = n < 10000 ? 200 : (n < 100000 ? 100 : 50);
        numStarts = Math.Min(numStarts, n);
        var tasks = new Task[numStarts];

        for (int i = 0; i < numStarts; i++)
        {
            int start = i;
            tasks[i] = Task.Run(() =>
            {
                var clique = GreedyClique(adj, start, cts.Token);
                lock (lockObj)
                {
                    if (clique.Count > bestClique.Count)
                        bestClique = clique;
                }
            }, cts.Token);
        }

        try
        {
            Task.WaitAll(tasks, cts.Token);
        }
        catch { }

        Console.WriteLine(bestClique.Count);
        if (bestClique.Count > 0)
        {
            Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", bestClique.OrderBy(x => x)));
        }
    }

    static List<int> GreedyClique(List<HashSet<int>> adj, int start, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var clique = new List<int> { start };
        var candidates = new HashSet<int>(adj[start]);

        while (candidates.Count > 0 && !ct.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            int best = -1;
            int bestScore = -1;

            foreach (var v in candidates)
            {
                int score = 0;
                foreach (var u in candidates)
                {
                    if (u != v && adj[v].Contains(u))
                        score++;
                }

                if (score > bestScore)
                {
                    bestScore = score;
                    best = v;
                }
            }

            clique.Add(best);
            candidates.RemoveWhere(v => v == best || !adj[best].Contains(v));
        }

        return clique;
    }
}

View C# Code (2539 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        var line = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        int n = int.Parse(line[0]);
        int m = int.Parse(line[1]);

        var adj = new List<HashSet<int>>(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            adj.Add(new HashSet<int>());

        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++)
        {
            var parts = Console.ReadLine().Split();
            int u = int.Parse(parts[0]);
            int v = int.Parse(parts[1]);
            adj[u].Add(v);
            adj[v].Add(u);
        }

        var cts = new CancellationTokenSource(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(270));
        List<int> bestClique = new();
        object lockObj = new();

        int numStarts = n < 10000 ? 200 : (n < 100000 ? 100 : 50);
        numStarts = Math.Min(numStarts, n);
        var tasks = new Task[numStarts];

        for (int i = 0; i < numStarts; i++)
        {
            int start = i;
            tasks[i] = Task.Run(() =>
            {
                var clique = GreedyClique(adj, start, cts.Token);
                lock (lockObj)
                {
                    if (clique.Count > bestClique.Count)
                        bestClique = clique;
                }
            }, cts.Token);
        }

        try
        {
            Task.WaitAll(tasks, cts.Token);
        }
        catch { }

        Console.WriteLine(bestClique.Count);
        if (bestClique.Count > 0)
        {
            Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", bestClique.OrderBy(x => x)));
        }
    }

    static List<int> GreedyClique(List<HashSet<int>> adj, int start, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var clique = new List<int> { start };
        var candidates = new HashSet<int>(adj[start]);

        while (candidates.Count > 0 && !ct.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            int best = -1;
            int bestScore = -1;

            foreach (var v in candidates)
            {
                int score = 0;
                foreach (var u in candidates)
                {
                    if (u != v && adj[v].Contains(u))
                        score++;
                }

                if (score > bestScore)
                {
                    bestScore = score;
                    best = v;
                }
            }

            clique.Add(best);
            candidates.RemoveWhere(v => v == best || !adj[best].Contains(v));
        }

        return clique;
    }
}

Test 30: 3-SAT Solver (Python) Score: 0.00 / 17
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Python code to solve the 3-SAT (Boolean Satisfiability) problem, which is NP-Complete.

**What is 3-SAT?**
You have N boolean variables (x1, x2, ..., xN), each of which is either True or False.
You are given M "clauses". Each clause is a constraint containing exactly 3 "literals".
A literal is either:
- A positive integer (e.g. 5) meaning "variable 5 must be True", or
- A negative integer (e.g. -5) meaning "variable 5 must be False".

A clause is **satisfied** if ANY of its 3 literals evaluates to True.
The formula is **satisfiable** if ALL clauses can be satisfied simultaneously
by some assignment of True/False to the variables.

**Example:**
Variables: x1, x2, x3
Clause "1 -2 3" means: x1=True OR x2=False OR x3=True.
If x1=True, x2=True, x3=False → clause satisfied (x1=True is enough).

Your task: find an assignment of True/False to every variable that satisfies
every clause. All test instances are guaranteed to be satisfiable.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_variables num_clauses
lit1 lit2 lit3
lit1 lit2 lit3
...
```
Variables are 1-indexed. Negative literals mean negation.

**Output format (stdout):**
```
SAT
v1 v2 v3 ... vN
```
Where each vi is 1 (True) or 0 (False) for variable i, space-separated.

**Grading:**
- Output "SAT" on the first line, then the assignment on the second line.
- Every clause must be satisfied by your assignment.
- Timeouts, crashes, wrong format, or unsatisfied clauses all score 0.

**Requirements:**
1. Read from stdin, write to stdout.
2. Handle up to 20M variables and 85M clauses.
3. Complete within 5 minutes.

Write complete, runnable Python code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a logic puzzle made of true/false variables — where each rule says “at least one of these three things must be true” and all rules must hold simultaneously — find an assignment that satisfies every rule.

This is the classic 3-SAT problem, one of the first problems proven to be NP-complete. Every test instance has a hidden satisfying assignment the AI must discover. Subpasses increase the number of variables and clauses.

 0.0000
[20 vars, 85 clauses] 3/85 clauses unsatisfied, 0.21s

3-SAT Solver - 20 vars, 85 clauses

Approach: !/usr/bin/env python3

View Python Code (3703 chars)
import sys
import random
from collections import defaultdict

def solve_sat():
    line = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    if not line:
        return

    parts = line.split()
    num_vars = int(parts[0])
    num_clauses = int(parts[1])

    clauses = []
    for _ in range(num_clauses):
        clause_line = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
        if clause_line:
            clause = list(map(int, clause_line.split()))
            clauses.append(clause)

    if num_clauses == 0:
        print("SAT")
        print(" ".join(["0"] * num_vars))
        return

    # WalkSAT algorithm with unit propagation
    assignment = [None] * (num_vars + 1)

    # Count literal frequencies for initial assignment
    lit_count = defaultdict(int)
    for clause in clauses:
        for lit in clause:
            lit_count[lit] += 1

    # Initial greedy assignment
    for var in range(1, num_vars + 1):
        assignment[var] = lit_count[var] >= lit_count[-var]

    # Local search phase
    max_flips = min(num_vars * 100, 10000000)
    for flip_count in range(max_flips):
        unsatisfied = []
        for i, clause in enumerate(clauses):
            satisfied = False
            for lit in clause:
                var = abs(lit)
                if (lit > 0 and assignment[var]) or (lit < 0 and not assignment[var]):
                    satisfied = True
                    break
            if not satisfied:
                unsatisfied.append(i)

        if not unsatisfied:
            print("SAT")
            result = " ".join(str(int(assignment[i])) for i in range(1, num_vars + 1))
            print(result)
            return

        # Probabilistic random restart
        if flip_count % 100 == 0 and flip_count > 0 and random.random() < 0.1:
            for var in range(1, num_vars + 1):
                assignment[var] = random.choice([True, False])
        else:
            # Pick an unsatisfied clause
            clause_idx = unsatisfied[flip_count % len(unsatisfied)]
            clause = clauses[clause_idx]

            # Find best variable to flip (appears in most unsatisfied clauses)
            best_var = None
            best_score = -1
            for lit in clause:
                var = abs(lit)
                score = sum(1 for c_idx in unsatisfied if any(abs(l) == var for l in clauses[c_idx]))
                if score > best_score:
                    best_score = score
                    best_var = var

            if best_var:
                assignment[best_var] = not assignment[best_var]
            else:
                var = abs(clause[flip_count % len(clause)])
                assignment[var] = not assignment[var]

    # Unit propagation fallback
    assignment_copy = assignment[:]
    changed = True
    iterations = 0

    while changed and iterations < 1000:
        iterations += 1
        changed = False
        for clause in clauses:
            unassigned_count = 0
            unassigned_lit = None
            satisfied = False

            for lit in clause:
                var = abs(lit)
                if (lit > 0 and assignment_copy[var]) or (lit < 0 and not assignment_copy[var]):
                    satisfied = True
                    break
                else:
                    unassigned_count += 1
                    unassigned_lit = lit

            if not satisfied and unassigned_count == 1:
                var = abs(unassigned_lit)
                assignment_copy[var] = (unassigned_lit > 0)
                changed = True

    # Final verification and output
    print("SAT")
    result = " ".join(str(int(assignment_copy[i])) for i in range(1, num_vars + 1))
    print(result)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve_sat()

Test 31: Subset Sum (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 17
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Rust code to solve the Subset Sum problem.

Your Rust subset sum solver will be tested with instances ranging from simple to very
complex sets. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL problem complexities.

Given a set of integers and a target value, determine if there exists a subset
that sums to exactly the target value. Simple sets (<100 numbers) you can probably
brute force, however this code will also need to run on sets with millions of
numbers. This is NP-Complete and requires sophisticated algorithms for larger instances:
additionally; threading, memory optimization, vectorization and parallelization are all
strongly suggested.

**Input format (stdin, space seperated decimal integers):**
```
num_numbers target
num1 num2 ... numN
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
YES or NO
[index1 index2 ... indexK] (only if YES, 0-indexed indices of subset elements)
```

**Implementation Hints:**
- Detect set complexity and choose appropriate algorithm
- Use efficient data structures: bitsets, hash sets for DP
- Implement adaptive quality vs speed tradeoffs
- For very large sets, focus on approximation algorithms
- Handle edge cases: empty set, zero target, all numbers add to target.
- Use fast I/O as the input size can approach 1gb.

**Success Criteria:**
- Correctly determine if subset sum exists
- If YES, provide valid subset indices
- Complete within time limit

**Failure Criteria:**
- Incorrect subset sum determination
- Invalid subset indices
- Timeout without conclusion

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with rustc
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle variable set sizes and target values
4. Complete within 5 minutes
5. Must handle varying subset sum complexities efficiently

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Be aware that default warnings are enabled and will cause a compilation failure,
so ensure that you write warning-free code.

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a list of numbers and a target total, find a subset of those numbers that adds up to exactly the target. It sounds simple, but with large lists the number of possible subsets is astronomical.

This is a classic NP-hard problem. The test uses planted instances with a known solution for strict pass/fail grading. Subpasses increase the list size and target magnitude.

 0.0000
[20 numbers, small values] Error: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '[2'

Subset Sum - 20 numbers, small values

View Rust Code (2102 chars)
use std::collections::{HashMap, HashSet};
use std::io::{self, BufRead};

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let _num_numbers: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let target: i64 = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    let second_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let numbers: Vec<i64> = second_line
        .split_whitespace()
        .map(|s| s.parse().unwrap())
        .collect();

    match solve_subset_sum(&numbers, target) {
        Some(mut indices) => {
            println!("YES");
            indices.sort_unstable();
            let indices_str: Vec<String> = indices.iter().map(|i| i.to_string()).collect();
            println!("[{}]", indices_str.join(" "));
        }
        None => println!("NO"),
    }
}

fn solve_subset_sum(numbers: &[i64], target: i64) -> Option<Vec<usize>> {
    if target == 0 {
        return Some(vec![]);
    }

    if numbers.is_empty() {
        return None;
    }

    for (i, &num) in numbers.iter().enumerate() {
        if num == target {
            return Some(vec![i]);
        }
    }

    let mut dp: HashMap<i64, (i64, usize)> = HashMap::new();
    let mut reachable: HashSet<i64> = HashSet::new();
    reachable.insert(0);

    for (i, &num) in numbers.iter().enumerate() {
        let current_reachable: Vec<i64> = reachable.iter().copied().collect();

        for sum in current_reachable {
            let new_sum = sum + num;

            if new_sum == target {
                let mut result = vec![i];
                let mut cur_sum = sum;
                while cur_sum != 0 {
                    let (prev_sum, idx) = dp[&cur_sum];
                    result.push(idx);
                    cur_sum = prev_sum;
                }
                return Some(result);
            }

            if !reachable.contains(&new_sum) {
                reachable.insert(new_sum);
                dp.insert(new_sum, (sum, i));
            }
        }
    }

    None
}

Test 32: Steiner Tree (C++) Score: 3.00 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C++ code to solve the Steiner Tree problem (NP-Hard).

**Problem:**
Given a weighted undirected graph and a set of terminal vertices, find a minimum-weight
tree that connects all terminals. The tree may include non-terminal (Steiner) vertices.

**Input (stdin):**
```
num_vertices num_edges num_terminals
u v weight (one edge per line, 0-indexed)
terminal_1 terminal_2 ... terminal_K
```

**Output (stdout):**
```
total_weight edge_count
u1 v1
u2 v2
...
```
Edges listed must form a connected, acyclic subgraph that includes all terminals.


**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

**Hints (not required):**
- Exact DP (Dreyfus-Wagner) for small terminal sets.
- MST / shortest-path heuristics for large graphs.
- Use adjacency lists and fast I/O.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Connect a set of important nodes in a weighted graph using the cheapest possible tree of edges. Unlike a minimum spanning tree, the solution may pass through intermediate nodes that aren’t in the required set if doing so saves cost — like building a road network that connects key cities via junctions.

This is NP-hard. The test uses planted instances with a known optimal tree for strict grading. Subpasses increase the graph size.

 1.0000
[20V, 5 terminals] Weight 14 (MST bound: 14), 0.48s

Steiner Tree - 20V, 5 terminals

View C++ Code (3181 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <set>

using namespace std;

const long long INF = 1e18;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(NULL);

    int n, m, k;
    cin >> n >> m >> k;

    vector<vector<pair<int, long long>>> adj(n);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        long long w;
        cin >> u >> v >> w;
        adj[u].push_back({v, w});
        adj[v].push_back({u, w});
    }

    vector<int> terminals(k);
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
        cin >> terminals[i];
    }

    if (k <= 1) {
        cout << "0 0\n";
        return 0;
    }

    // Dreyfus-Wagner algorithm with edge tracking
    vector<vector<long long>> dp(1 << k, vector<long long>(n, INF));
    vector<vector<set<pair<int, int>>>> edges(1 << k, vector<set<pair<int, int>>>(n));

    // Initialize: single terminals have cost 0
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
        dp[1 << i][terminals[i]] = 0;
    }

    // Process all subsets of terminals
    for (int mask = 1; mask < (1 << k); mask++) {
        // Step 1: Combine two non-empty subsets at each vertex
        for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
            for (int sub = (mask - 1) & mask; sub > 0; sub = (sub - 1) & mask) {
                int comp = mask ^ sub;
                if (dp[sub][v] < INF && dp[comp][v] < INF) {
                    long long cost = dp[sub][v] + dp[comp][v];
                    if (cost < dp[mask][v]) {
                        dp[mask][v] = cost;
                        edges[mask][v].clear();
                        edges[mask][v].insert(edges[sub][v].begin(), edges[sub][v].end());
                        edges[mask][v].insert(edges[comp][v].begin(), edges[comp][v].end());
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // Step 2: Dijkstra relaxation for this subset
        priority_queue<pair<long long, int>, vector<pair<long long, int>>, greater<>> pq;
        vector<bool> visited(n, false);

        for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
            if (dp[mask][v] < INF) {
                pq.push({dp[mask][v], v});
            }
        }

        while (!pq.empty()) {
            auto [d, u] = pq.top();
            pq.pop();

            if (visited[u]) continue;
            visited[u] = true;

            for (auto [v, w] : adj[u]) {
                if (dp[mask][u] + w < dp[mask][v]) {
                    dp[mask][v] = dp[mask][u] + w;
                    edges[mask][v] = edges[mask][u];
                    int a = min(u, v), b = max(u, v);
                    edges[mask][v].insert({a, b});
                    pq.push({dp[mask][v], v});
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Find the minimum cost Steiner tree
    int full_mask = (1 << k) - 1;
    long long min_cost = INF;
    int best_root = -1;
    for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
        if (dp[full_mask][v] < min_cost) {
            min_cost = dp[full_mask][v];
            best_root = v;
        }
    }

    auto& result_edges = edges[full_mask][best_root];

    cout << min_cost << " " << result_edges.size() << "\n";
    for (auto [u, v] : result_edges) {
        cout << u << " " << v << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Valid

using namespace std;

const long long INF = 1e18;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(NULL);

    int n, m, k;
    cin >> n >> m >> k;

    vector<vector<pair<int, long long>>> adj(n);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        long long w;

        adj[v].push_back({u, w});

        cin >> terminals[i];
    }

    if (k <= 1) {
        cout << "0 0\n";
        return 0;
    }

    // Dreyfus-Wagner algorithm with edge tracking
    vector<vector<long long>> dp(1 << k, vector<long long>(n, INF));
    vector<vector<set<pair<int, int>>>> edges(1 << k, vector<set<pair<int, int>>>(n));

    // Initialize: single terminals have cost 0
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
        dp[1 << i][terminals[i]] = 0;
    }

    // Process all subsets of terminals
    for (int mask = 1; mask < (1 << k); mask++) {
        // Step 1: Combine two non-empty subsets at each vertex
        for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
            for (int sub = (mask - 1) & mask; sub > 0; sub = (sub - 1) & mask) {
                int comp = mask ^ sub;
                if (dp[sub][v] < INF && dp[comp][v] < INF) {
                    long long cost = dp[sub][v] + dp[comp][v];
                    if (cost < dp[mask][v]) {
                        dp[mask][v] = cost;
                        edges[mask][v].clear();
                        edges[mask][v].insert(edges[sub][v].begin(), edges[sub][v].end());
                        edges[mask][v].insert(edges[comp][v].begin(), edges[comp][v].end());
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // Step 2: Dijkstra relaxation for this subset
        priority_queue<pair<long long, int>, vector<pair<long long, int>>, greater<>> pq;
        vector<bool> visited(n, false);

        for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
            if (dp[mask][v] < INF) {
                pq.push({dp[mask][v], v});
            }
        }

        while (!pq.empty()) {
            auto [d, u] = pq.top();
            pq.pop();

            if (visited[u]) continue;
            visited[u] = true;

            for (auto [v, w] : adj[u]) {
                if (dp[mask][u] + w < dp[mask][v]) {
                    dp[mask][v] = dp[mask][u] + w;
                    edges[mask][v] = edges[mask][u];
                    int a = min(u, v), b = max(u, v);
                    edges[mask][v].insert({a, b});
                    pq.push({dp[mask][v], v});
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Find the minimum cost Steiner tree
    int full_mask = (1 << k) - 1;
    long long min_cost = INF;
    int best_root = -1;
    for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
        if (dp[full_mask][v] < min_cost) {
            min_cost = dp[full_mask][v];
            best_root = v;
        }
    }

    auto& result_edges = edges[full_mask][best_root];

    cout << min_cost << " " << result_edges.size() << "\n";
    for (auto [u, v] : result_edges) {
        cout << u << " " << v << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

        priority_queue<pair<long long, int>, vector<pair<long long, int>>, greater<>> pq;
        vector<bool> visited(n, false);

        for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {

#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <set>

using namespace std;

const long long INF = 1e18;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(NULL);

    int n, m, k;
    cin >> n >> m >> k;

    vector<vector<pair<int, long long>>> adj(n);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        long long w;
        cin >> u >> v >> w;
        adj[u].push_back({v, w});
        adj[v].push_back({u, w});
    }

    vector<int> terminals(k);
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
        cin >> terminals[i];
    }

    if (k <= 1) {
        cout << "0 0\n";
        return 0;
    }

    // Dreyfus-Wagner algorithm with edge tracking
    vector<vector<long long>> dp(1 << k, vector<long long>(n, INF));
    vector<vector<set<pair<int, int>>>> edges(1 << k, vector<set<pair<int, int>>>(n));

    // Initialize: single terminals have cost 0
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
        dp[1 << i][terminals[i]] = 0;
    }

    // Process all subsets of terminals
    for (int mask = 1; mask < (1 << k); mask++) {
        // Step 1: Combine two non-empty subsets at each vertex
        for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
            for (int sub = (mask - 1) & mask; sub > 0; sub = (sub - 1) & mask) {
                int comp = mask ^ sub;
                if (dp[sub][v] < INF && dp[comp][v] < INF) {
                    long long cost = dp[sub][v] + dp[comp][v];
                    if (cost < dp[mask][v]) {
                        dp[mask][v] = cost;
                        edges[mask][v].clear();
                        edges[mask][v].insert(edges[sub][v].begin(), edges[sub][v].end());
                        edges[mask][v].insert(edges[comp][v].begin(), edges[comp][v].end());
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // Step 2: Dijkstra relaxation for this subset
        priority_queue<pair<long long, int>, vector<pair<long long, int>>, greater<>> pq;
        vector<bool> visited(n, false);

        for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
            if (dp[mask][v] < INF) {
                pq.push({dp[mask][v], v});
            }
        }

        while (!pq.empty()) {
            auto [d, u] = pq.top();
            pq.pop();

            if (visited[u]) continue;
            visited[u] = true;

            for (auto [v, w] : adj[u]) {
                if (dp[mask][u] + w < dp[mask][v]) {
                    dp[mask][v] = dp[mask][u] + w;
                    edges[mask][v] = edges[mask][u];
                    int a = min(u, v), b = max(u, v);
                    edges[mask][v].insert({a, b});
                    pq.push({dp[mask][v], v});
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Find the minimum cost Steiner tree
    int full_mask = (1 << k) - 1;
    long long min_cost = INF;
    int best_root = -1;
    for (int v = 0; v < n; v++) {
        if (dp[full_mask][v] < min_cost) {
            min_cost = dp[full_mask][v];
            best_root = v;
        }
    }

    auto& result_edges = edges[full_mask][best_root];

    cout << min_cost << " " << result_edges.size() << "\n";
    for (auto [u, v] : result_edges) {
        cout << u << " " << v << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 33: Quadratic Assignment Problem (C#) Score: 0.00 / 17
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C# code to solve the Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP), which is NP-hard.

**Problem:**
You have N facilities and N locations. You must assign each facility to exactly one
location (a permutation). The cost of assigning facility i to location p[i] is:

sum over all pairs (i, j): flow[i][j] * distance[p[i]][p[j]]

Your goal is to minimize this total cost.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
N
flow[0][0] flow[0][1] ... flow[0][N-1]
...
flow[N-1][0] ... flow[N-1][N-1]
dist[0][0] dist[0][1] ... dist[0][N-1]
...
dist[N-1][0] ... dist[N-1][N-1]
```
All values are integers. Matrices are N×N. Indices are 0-based in the output.

**Output format (stdout):**
```
total_cost
p0 p1 p2 ... p(N-1)
```
Where p is a permutation of 0..N-1 and p[i] is the location assigned to facility i.

**Requirements:**
1. Read from stdin, write to stdout.
2. Output a valid permutation.
3. Handle N from very small up to ~2000 within 5 minutes.
4. Heuristics are acceptable for large N; lower cost scores higher.

Write complete, compilable C# code with a Main method. Assume only the standard library.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Assign N facilities to N locations so that the total cost is minimised, where cost depends on how much material flows between each pair of facilities multiplied by the distance between their assigned locations. Think of arranging departments in an office to minimise how far people walk between them.

This is the Quadratic Assignment Problem, one of the hardest combinatorial optimisation problems. The test uses planted instances with a known optimum for strict grading. Subpasses increase the problem size.

 0.0000
Compilation error: Compilation failed:
Determining projects to restore...
Restored D:\CodingBenchmark\compile_cache\claude-haiku-4-5-LowReasoning\csharp\project_4c95fbb603c412ce\Program.csproj (in 238 ms).
CSC : err

Quadratic Assignment - 8 facilities

View C# Code (5377 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

class Program
{
    static Random rand = new Random(42);

    static void Main()
    {
        int n = int.Parse(Console.ReadLine());

        int[][] flow = new int[n][];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            flow[i] = Console.ReadLine().Split().Select(int.Parse).ToArray();
        }

        int[][] distance = new int[n][];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            distance[i] = Console.ReadLine().Split().Select(int.Parse).ToArray();
        }

        int[] bestPerm = new int[n];
        long bestCost;

        if (n <= 8)
        {
            bestPerm = BruteForceQAP(n, flow, distance, out bestCost);
        }
        else
        {
            bestCost = long.MaxValue;
            int numTries = n <= 100 ? 10 : n <= 500 ? 5 : 2;

            for (int t = 0; t < numTries; t++)
            {
                int[] perm = (t == 0) ? GreedyQAP(n, flow, distance) : RandomPermutation(n);
                long cost = CalculateCost(perm, flow, distance);

                LocalSearchSwap(perm, flow, distance, ref cost);

                if (cost < bestCost)
                {
                    bestCost = cost;
                    bestPerm = perm;
                }
            }
        }

        Console.WriteLine(bestCost);
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", bestPerm));
    }

    static int[] BruteForceQAP(int n, int[][] flow, int[][] distance, out long bestCost)
    {
        int[] bestPerm = Enumerable.Range(0, n).ToArray();
        bestCost = CalculateCost(bestPerm, flow, distance);

        int[] perm = (int[])bestPerm.Clone();
        while (NextPermutation(perm))
        {
            long cost = CalculateCost(perm, flow, distance);
            if (cost < bestCost)
            {
                bestCost = cost;
                bestPerm = (int[])perm.Clone();
            }
        }

        return bestPerm;
    }

    static int[] GreedyQAP(int n, int[][] flow, int[][] distance)
    {
        int[] perm = new int[n];
        bool[] used = new bool[n];

        perm[0] = 0;
        used[0] = true;

        for (int i = 1; i < n; i++)
        {
            int bestLoc = -1;
            long bestCost = long.MaxValue;

            for (int loc = 0; loc < n; loc++)
            {
                if (used[loc]) continue;

                long totalCost = 0;
                for (int j = 0; j < i; j++)
                {
                    totalCost += (long)flow[j][i] * distance[perm[j]][loc];
                    totalCost += (long)flow[i][j] * distance[loc][perm[j]];
                }

                if (totalCost < bestCost)
                {
                    bestCost = totalCost;
                    bestLoc = loc;
                }
            }

            perm[i] = bestLoc;
            used[bestLoc] = true;
        }

        return perm;
    }

    static int[] RandomPermutation(int n)
    {
        int[] perm = Enumerable.Range(0, n).ToArray();
        for (int i = n - 1; i > 0; i--)
        {
            int j = rand.Next(i + 1);
            int temp = perm[i];
            perm[i] = perm[j];
            perm[j] = temp;
        }
        return perm;
    }

    static void LocalSearchSwap(int[] perm, int[][] flow, int[][] distance, ref long currentCost)
    {
        int n = perm.Length;
        bool improved = true;
        int iterations = 0;
        int maxIterations = n > 500 ? 5 : n > 100 ? 20 : 100;

        while (improved && iterations < maxIterations)
        {
            improved = false;
            iterations++;

            for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++)
            {
                for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++)
                {
                    int temp = perm[i];
                    perm[i] = perm[j];
                    perm[j] = temp;

                    long newCost = CalculateCost(perm, flow, distance);
                    if (newCost < currentCost)
                    {
                        currentCost = newCost;
                        improved = true;
                        break;
                    }
                    else
                    {
                        perm[j] = perm[i];
                        perm[i] = temp;
                    }
                }
                if (improved) break;
            }
        }
    }

    static long CalculateCost(int[] perm, int[][] flow, int[][] distance)
    {
        long cost = 0;
        int n = perm.Length;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
            {
                cost += (long)flow[i][j] * distance[perm[i]][perm[j]];
            }
        }
        return cost;
    }

    static void Reverse(int[] arr, int start, int end)
    {
        while (start < end)
        {
            int temp = arr[start];
            arr[start] = arr[end];
            arr[end] = temp;
            start++;
            end--;
        }
    }

    static bool NextPermutation(int[] arr)
    {
        int i = arr.Length - 2;
        while (i >= 0 && arr[i] >= arr[i + 1]) i--;

        if (i < 0) return false;

        int j = arr.Length - 1;
        while (j > i && arr[j] <= arr[i]) j--;

        int temp = arr[i];
        arr[i] = arr[j];
        arr[j] = temp;

        Reverse(arr, i + 1, arr.Length - 1);
        return true;
    }
}

Test 34: Vehicle Routing Problem (Python) Score: 1.00 / 17
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Python code to solve the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP).

**Problem:**
You have a depot and N customers with coordinates and demands. You must create up to K vehicle
routes starting and ending at the depot. Each customer must be visited exactly once and the sum
of demands on any route must not exceed the vehicle capacity. Minimize total travel distance.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
N K CAPACITY
depot_x depot_y
x1 y1 demand1
x2 y2 demand2
...
xN yN demandN
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
total_distance
num_routes
route_1
route_2
...
```
Each route line should list customer indices (0-based) in visit order.

**Requirements:**
1. Read from stdin, write to stdout.
2. Output valid routes that cover all customers exactly once.
3. Respect vehicle capacity on every route.
4. Heuristics are acceptable for large instances; shorter distance scores higher.

Write complete, runnable Python code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Plan delivery routes for a fleet of vehicles so that every customer is visited and the total distance travelled is minimised. Each vehicle has a capacity limit, so the AI must decide which customers go on which truck and in what order.

This is the Vehicle Routing Problem, a generalisation of the Travelling Salesman Problem. Subpasses increase the number of customers and vehicles. The baseline uses a simple nearest-neighbour heuristic.

 1.0000
[15 customers, 3 vehicles] Distance 671.6 (NN: 678.6), 0.45s

Vehicle Routing - 15 customers, 3 vehicles

View Python Code (5243 chars)
import sys
import math
from typing import List, Tuple

def distance(p1: Tuple[float, float], p2: Tuple[float, float]) -> float:
    return math.sqrt((p1[0] - p2[0])**2 + (p1[1] - p2[1])**2)

def calculate_route_distance(route: List[int], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> float:
    total = distance(depot, customers[route[0]])
    for i in range(len(route) - 1):
        total += distance(customers[route[i]], customers[route[i+1]])
    total += distance(customers[route[-1]], depot)
    return total

def calculate_total_distance(routes: List[List[int]], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> float:
    return sum(calculate_route_distance(route, depot, customers) for route in routes)

def nearest_neighbor_cvrp(depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]], demands: List[int], capacity: int, num_vehicles: int) -> List[List[int]]:
    n = len(customers)
    unvisited = set(range(n))
    routes = []

    while unvisited:
        route = []
        current_load = 0
        current_pos = depot

        while unvisited:
            nearest = None
            nearest_dist = float('inf')

            for customer in unvisited:
                if current_load + demands[customer] <= capacity:
                    dist = distance(current_pos, customers[customer])
                    if dist < nearest_dist:
                        nearest_dist = dist
                        nearest = customer

            if nearest is None:
                break

            route.append(nearest)
            current_load += demands[nearest]
            current_pos = customers[nearest]
            unvisited.remove(nearest)

        if route:
            routes.append(route)

        if not unvisited:
            break

    return routes

def two_opt_within_route(route: List[int], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> List[int]:
    improved = True
    best_route = route[:]

    while improved:
        improved = False
        for i in range(len(best_route) - 1):
            for j in range(i + 2, len(best_route)):
                new_route = best_route[:i+1] + best_route[i+1:j+1][::-1] + best_route[j+1:]

                old_dist = calculate_route_distance(best_route, depot, customers)
                new_dist = calculate_route_distance(new_route, depot, customers)

                if new_dist < old_dist:
                    best_route = new_route
                    improved = True
                    break
            if improved:
                break

    return best_route

def two_opt_improve(routes: List[List[int]], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> List[List[int]]:
    improved_routes = [two_opt_within_route(route, depot, customers) for route in routes]
    return improved_routes

def merge_small_routes(routes: List[List[int]], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]], demands: List[int], capacity: int) -> List[List[int]]:
    if len(routes) <= 1:
        return routes

    merged = True
    while merged:
        merged = False
        for i in range(len(routes)):
            for j in range(i + 1, len(routes)):
                route_i_load = sum(demands[c] for c in routes[i])
                route_j_load = sum(demands[c] for c in routes[j])

                if route_i_load + route_j_load <= capacity:
                    merged_route = routes[i] + routes[j]
                    merged_route = two_opt_within_route(merged_route, depot, customers)

                    old_dist = calculate_route_distance(routes[i], depot, customers) + calculate_route_distance(routes[j], depot, customers)
                    new_dist = calculate_route_distance(merged_route, depot, customers)

                    if new_dist < old_dist:
                        routes[i] = merged_route
                        routes.pop(j)
                        merged = True
                        break
            if merged:
                break

    return routes

def solve_cvrp(n: int, k: int, capacity: int, depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]], demands: List[int]) -> Tuple[float, List[List[int]]]:
    best_routes = nearest_neighbor_cvrp(depot, customers, demands, capacity, k)
    best_routes = two_opt_improve(best_routes, depot, customers)
    best_routes = merge_small_routes(best_routes, depot, customers, demands, capacity)
    best_distance = calculate_total_distance(best_routes, depot, customers)

    return best_distance, best_routes

def main():
    lines = sys.stdin.read().strip().split('\n')
    n, k, capacity = map(int, lines[0].split())
    depot_x, depot_y = map(float, lines[1].split())
    depot = (depot_x, depot_y)

    customers = []
    demands = []

    for i in range(2, 2 + n):
        parts = lines[i].split()
        x, y = float(parts[0]), float(parts[1])
        demand = int(parts[2])
        customers.append((x, y))
        demands.append(demand)

    total_distance, routes = solve_cvrp(n, k, capacity, depot, customers, demands)

    print(f"{total_distance:.2f}")
    print(len(routes))
    for route in routes:
        print(' '.join(map(str, route)))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
VRP Visualization — VALID (distance 671.6)

View Python Code (5243 chars)
import sys
import math
from typing import List, Tuple

def distance(p1: Tuple[float, float], p2: Tuple[float, float]) -> float:
    return math.sqrt((p1[0] - p2[0])**2 + (p1[1] - p2[1])**2)

def calculate_route_distance(route: List[int], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> float:
    total = distance(depot, customers[route[0]])
    for i in range(len(route) - 1):
        total += distance(customers[route[i]], customers[route[i+1]])
    total += distance(customers[route[-1]], depot)
    return total

def calculate_total_distance(routes: List[List[int]], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> float:
    return sum(calculate_route_distance(route, depot, customers) for route in routes)

def nearest_neighbor_cvrp(depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]], demands: List[int], capacity: int, num_vehicles: int) -> List[List[int]]:
    n = len(customers)
    unvisited = set(range(n))
    routes = []

    while unvisited:
        route = []
        current_load = 0
        current_pos = depot

        while unvisited:
            nearest = None
            nearest_dist = float('inf')

            for customer in unvisited:
                if current_load + demands[customer] <= capacity:
                    dist = distance(current_pos, customers[customer])
                    if dist < nearest_dist:
                        nearest_dist = dist
                        nearest = customer

            if nearest is None:
                break

            route.append(nearest)
            current_load += demands[nearest]
            current_pos = customers[nearest]
            unvisited.remove(nearest)

        if route:
            routes.append(route)

        if not unvisited:
            break

    return routes

def two_opt_within_route(route: List[int], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> List[int]:
    improved = True
    best_route = route[:]

    while improved:
        improved = False
        for i in range(len(best_route) - 1):
            for j in range(i + 2, len(best_route)):
                new_route = best_route[:i+1] + best_route[i+1:j+1][::-1] + best_route[j+1:]

                old_dist = calculate_route_distance(best_route, depot, customers)
                new_dist = calculate_route_distance(new_route, depot, customers)

                if new_dist < old_dist:
                    best_route = new_route
                    improved = True
                    break
            if improved:
                break

    return best_route

def two_opt_improve(routes: List[List[int]], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]]) -> List[List[int]]:
    improved_routes = [two_opt_within_route(route, depot, customers) for route in routes]
    return improved_routes

def merge_small_routes(routes: List[List[int]], depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]], demands: List[int], capacity: int) -> List[List[int]]:
    if len(routes) <= 1:
        return routes

    merged = True
    while merged:
        merged = False
        for i in range(len(routes)):
            for j in range(i + 1, len(routes)):
                route_i_load = sum(demands[c] for c in routes[i])
                route_j_load = sum(demands[c] for c in routes[j])

                if route_i_load + route_j_load <= capacity:
                    merged_route = routes[i] + routes[j]
                    merged_route = two_opt_within_route(merged_route, depot, customers)

                    old_dist = calculate_route_distance(routes[i], depot, customers) + calculate_route_distance(routes[j], depot, customers)
                    new_dist = calculate_route_distance(merged_route, depot, customers)

                    if new_dist < old_dist:
                        routes[i] = merged_route
                        routes.pop(j)
                        merged = True
                        break
            if merged:
                break

    return routes

def solve_cvrp(n: int, k: int, capacity: int, depot: Tuple[float, float], customers: List[Tuple[float, float]], demands: List[int]) -> Tuple[float, List[List[int]]]:
    best_routes = nearest_neighbor_cvrp(depot, customers, demands, capacity, k)
    best_routes = two_opt_improve(best_routes, depot, customers)
    best_routes = merge_small_routes(best_routes, depot, customers, demands, capacity)
    best_distance = calculate_total_distance(best_routes, depot, customers)

    return best_distance, best_routes

def main():
    lines = sys.stdin.read().strip().split('\n')
    n, k, capacity = map(int, lines[0].split())
    depot_x, depot_y = map(float, lines[1].split())
    depot = (depot_x, depot_y)

    customers = []
    demands = []

    for i in range(2, 2 + n):
        parts = lines[i].split()
        x, y = float(parts[0]), float(parts[1])
        demand = int(parts[2])
        customers.append((x, y))
        demands.append(demand)

    total_distance, routes = solve_cvrp(n, k, capacity, depot, customers, demands)

    print(f"{total_distance:.2f}")
    print(len(routes))
    for route in routes:
        print(' '.join(map(str, route)))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Test 35: Minimum Dominating Set (Rust) Score: 14.00 / 21
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Rust code to solve the Minimum Dominating Set problem (NP-hard).

**Problem:**
Given an undirected graph, find a small set of vertices S such that every vertex is
either in S or adjacent to at least one vertex in S. Smaller sets score better.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_vertices num_edges
u1 v1
u2 v2
...
```
Vertices are 0-indexed.

**Output format (stdout):**
```
set_size
v1 v2 v3 ...
```
Where the second line lists the vertices in the dominating set (0-indexed).

**Requirements:**
1. Read from stdin, write to stdout.
2. Output a valid dominating set.
3. Heuristics are acceptable for large graphs; smaller sets score higher.
4. Must run within 5 minutes.

**Target environment specs**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler information**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc


Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find the smallest set of nodes such that every node in the graph is either in the set or directly connected to a node in the set. Think of placing Wi-Fi routers so that every room is within range of at least one router.

This is NP-hard. The test uses planted instances with a known optimal set for strict pass/fail grading. Subpasses increase the graph size.

 1.0000
[20 vertices] Size 3 (greedy: 3), 0.04s

Minimum Dominating Set - 20 vertices

View Rust Code (2225 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};
use std::collections::HashSet;

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read first line
    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let num_vertices: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let num_edges: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    // Build adjacency list
    let mut adj: Vec<HashSet<usize>> = vec![HashSet::new(); num_vertices];

    for _ in 0..num_edges {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
        let u: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
        let v: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
        adj[u].insert(v);
        adj[v].insert(u);
    }

    // Greedy dominating set algorithm
    let mut dominating_set = HashSet::new();
    let mut covered = HashSet::new();

    while covered.len() < num_vertices {
        // Find vertex not yet in dominating set that covers most uncovered vertices
        let mut best_vertex = 0;
        let mut best_count = 0;

        for v in 0..num_vertices {
            if dominating_set.contains(&v) {
                continue;
            }

            let mut count = 0;
            if !covered.contains(&v) {
                count += 1; // The vertex itself
            }
            for neighbor in &adj[v] {
                if !covered.contains(neighbor) {
                    count += 1;
                }
            }

            if count > best_count {
                best_count = count;
                best_vertex = v;
            }
        }

        // Add best vertex to dominating set
        dominating_set.insert(best_vertex);
        covered.insert(best_vertex);
        for neighbor in &adj[best_vertex] {
            covered.insert(*neighbor);
        }
    }

    // Output
    let mut result: Vec<usize> = dominating_set.iter().cloned().collect();
    result.sort();

    println!("{}", result.len());
    if !result.is_empty() {
        let output = result.iter()
            .map(|x| x.to_string())
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(" ");
        println!("{}", output);
    }
}
